raw_text: LABORDE, Rev. H. W.—Rector of St. George's and St. Andrew's parishes, and chaplain of the gaol, St. Vincent, appointed 1852; M.A. Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1845; was ordained assistant curate in St. Vincent, 1845; was minister of All Saints' chapel and garrison, chaplain of the island of Trinidad, 1850 to 1852; is chaplain to the Bishop of Barbados; was appointed the rural dean of St. Vincent, 1864; and also a member of the legislative council.
LABUAN, 2nd BISHOP of. Consecrated, July 29th, 1869.—RIGHT REV. DR. CHAMBERS.—For many years archdeacon of Sarawak.

LAIRD, DAVID.—Born 1833; sat in Prince Edward Island Assembly from 1871 to 1873, when returned to the dominion house of commons, where he still sits. Was a member of the executive council, P. E. I., from Nov. 1872, to April, 1873, and whilst holding that position negotiated for the entrance of the colony into the dominion; sworn of the privy council, and appointed minister of the interior of the dominion, 7th Nov., 1873; lieutenant-governor of the north-west territories, 7th Oct., 1876.
LAKE, ALEXANDER.—A solicitor in Jamaica; clerk of the peace for St. Mary's, and clerk of the St. Ann's district court, Jamaica.

raw_text: LANGEVIN, EDOUARD JOSEPH, N.P.—Admitted as a notary public, Lower Canada, 3rd Dec., 1858; a member of board of notaries of Lower Canada for several years; appointed clerk of the Crown in Chancery, Canada, 4th Jan., 1865; to same office for dominion, 5th July, 1867; deputy registrar-general, 1st July, 1868; under secretary of state of Canada, 9th July, 1873; was major 9th Batt. V.M., or Voltigeurs de Québec, and retired retaining rank, 1865.

LANGEVIN, HECTOR LOUIS, C.B. (1868), Q.C. —Born, 1826; called to the bar of Lower Canada, 1850; created a queen's counsel, 1864; mayor of Quebec from 1858 to 1861, and as such received H.R.H. the Prince of Wales on his landing in Canada, 1860; entered Canadian parliament, 1857, and has almost uninterruptedly held a seat up to the present time, first in the old legislative assembly, and since confederation, in the house of commons of the dominion; appointed a member of the executive council, Canada, and solicitor-general for Lower Canada, 30th March, 1864; postmaster-general, Nov., 1866; at confederation, 1st July, 1867, sworn of the queen's privy council and appointed secretary of state of Canada; minister of public works, 8th Dec., 1869, and whilst holding that office was elected as the successor of the late Sir G. E. Cartier, Bart., as leader of the liberal conservative party of Lower Canada; resigned office, 5th Nov., 1873; was one of the framers of the present Canadian constitution, and in furtherance of the cause of confederation attended the colonial conferences at Charlottetown and Quebec in 1864, and in London in 1866, when the Act of Union was carried through the imperial parliament; created a Knight Commander of the Roman Order of St. Gregory the Great, 1870; is the author of a prize essay on Canada, and of several other works.

LANGTON, JOHN, M.A.—Sat in Canadian parliament from 1851 to 1855, when appointed auditor of public accounts for province; appointed auditor of the dominion, 30th May, 1868; and subsequently, deputy of the minister of finance, and secretary of the treasury board; declined the finance ministry, 1862; has been vice-chancellor of the University of Toronto.

LANYON, MAJOR W. OWEN, C.M.G., (1875).— Aide-de-camp to the major-general commanding the forces, Jamaica, Aug. 1865, till March, 1868; aide-de-camp to Sir J. P. Grant, K.C.B., governor of Jamaica, April, 1868; appointed private secretary, August, 1868; volunteered for service on Gold Coast 1873; for some time acted as colonial secretary; afterwards aide-de-camp to Sir G. Wolseley; despatched on special service to the Gold Coast to act as colonial secretary, November, 1874; sat in the executive and legislative councils; promoted to be a regimental major, April, 1874; administrator of the government, Griqualand West 1875.

LAURENCE, E. H.— Resident magistrate, Greenough, Western Australia, March, 1870; was clerk of councils, and private secretary to the acting governor, Nov. 1868, to Sept. 1869; clerk in governor's office and various departments from March, 1863.

raw_text: LAYARD, SIR C. P., K.C.M.G. (1876), (C.M.G., 1871).—Was formerly at St. John's College, Cambridge; appointed extra-assistant in the colonial secretary's office in Ceylon, 1830; fiscal and magistrate at Jaffna, 1831; assistant to the collector of Colombo, 1832; assistant government agent and district judge, at Kalutara, 1836; assistant government agent and district judge, Negombo, 1837; district judge, Galle, 1839; district judge of Trincomalee, 1840; district judge of Galle, 1850; government agent, western province, 1851; held acting appointment of colonial secretary in conjunction with his own office of government agent for the western province from April to 30th June, 1869, and again from 6th April to 5th Sept. 1871.

LAYARD, EDGAR LEOPOLD, C.M.G., (1875).— Whilst Her Majesty's consul in Fiji, was concerned in the negotiations which resulted in the cession of the islands to Great Britain; administered the government from the cession, in 1874, until the arrival of Sir A. Gordon, in 1875.

raw_text: LEES, C. C., C.M.G. (1876).—Was private secretary and aide-de-camp to the governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, had local rank of lieut.-colonel, and brigade-major to the militia; entered the army in June, 1854; appointed adjutant to the 2nd battalion Royal Welch Fusiliers on its formation in 1858; sold out in Jan. 1866; and was appointed to the adjutancy of the 3rd administrative battalion Derbyshire rifle volunteers; resigned in May, 1867; appointed collector of customs, Lagos, Nov. 1872; administrator of the government of Lagos, July, 1874, and also lieutenant-governor of the Gold Coast Colony, Nov. 1874; administered the general government, 1876.

raw_text: LEFEVRE, K.C.B. (Civil). (Creat. 1857.) SIR JOHN GEORGE SHAW LEFEVRE.—Educated at Eton and Trinity College, Camb., where he was senior wrangler, 1818; fellow of Trinity, 1819; graduated M.A. 1821; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1824; elected a bencher, 1855; appointed under-secretary of state for the colonies, 1833; poor-law commissioner, 1834; joint-assistant secretary to the board of trade, 1841; deputy-clerk of the parliaments, 1848; clerk of the parliaments, 1858; is an ecclesiastical commissioner, and vice-chancellor of the university of London; was a civil service commissioner, but resigned 1862.

raw_text: LEFROY, MAJOR-GENERAL JOHN HENRY, R.A., C.B. (Civil, 1870), F.R.S., &c.—Entered the Royal Artillery, 1834; director of the magnetical and meteorological observatory at St. Helena, 1840-1; at Toronto, 1842-1853; made a magnetic survey of the interior of the continent of North America from Montreal to the Arctic Circle, 1843-4; appointed to the War office as scientific adviser to the Duke of Newcastle on subjects of artillery and inventions, Dec. 1854; sent on special mission to the seat of war, Oct. 1855; and to the Mediterranean fortresses, March, 1859; inspector-general of army schools, 1857-9; secretary ordnance select committee, 1859-63; president, 1864-8; director-general of ordnance, 1868-70; honorary secretary royal commission of the Patriotic Fund, 1854-68; commissioner, 1868; was also a member of the royal commission on national defences, 1859; and on military education, 1868; appointed governor of Bermuda, 1871.

raw_text: LOVETT, WILLIAM.—Auditor, Tasmania; appointed to post-office, February, 1841; accountant to colonial treasury, 1860; deputy auditor, April, 1863; assistant colonial treasurer, January, 1867.

LOW, HUGH.—Entered the colonial service as secretary to government of Labuan in 1848; police magistrate, 1850; administered the government of Labuan in 1855 and in 1863, and from Oct. to Dec., 1865; again from Nov. 1866, to Dec., 1867, and also from Dec., 1874, until April, 1876; British resident, Perak, 1877.

LOW, WILLIAM.—Acting private secretary to Lieut.-Governor Longden at Dominica, June, 1866, to March, 1867; private secretary to Lieutenant-Governor Bulwer and clerk of the patents, April, 1867, to January, 1869; and to Lieut.-Governor Freeling from February to July, 1869, acted as colonial secretary of Dominica, 1868; police magistrate in St. Vincent, May, 1874.

raw_text: LOWE, ALEX. C.—Private secretary to the president of Turks Islands 1849; was also a member of the board of education, and honorary secretary and treasurer of the same; was a justice of the peace, and captain and adjutant in the Turks Islands volunteer corps; colonial secretary, April, 1866, and a member of executive council, March, 1867; appointed receiver-general and treasurer, Bahamas, 1873.

LOWTHER, JAMES, M.P.—Born 1840. Educated at Westminster School, and Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1862, and M.A., 1866; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1864; is a magistrate and deputy lieutenant for the North Riding of York; was parliamentary secretary to the poor law board from August to December 1868; appointed parliamentary under-secretary for the colonies, February, 1874. First elected for the city of York, July, 1865.

raw_text: LUCAS, G. A.—Was a captain in the 73rd regiment; appointed resident magistrate, Lower Umcomas, Natal, 1859; magistrate Klip River in that colony, 1860, and at Alexandra, 1875.

LUDLOW, HENRY.—Called to the bar, Lincoln's Inn, January, 1862; attained studentship, 1861. Employed in Friendly Society's commission, and also under the parliamentary counsel; attorney-general, Trinidad, May, 1874.

MACALISTER, ARTHUR, C.M.G. (1876).—Formerly colonial secretary and premier of Queensland, now agent-general for the colony in London.

events.text_span: SIR WILLIAM MACARTHUR.—Was an elective member of the legislative council of New South Wales from 1848 to 1855

raw_text: MACARTHUR, KNT. BACHEL. (creat. 1856).—SIR WILLIAM MACARTHUR.—Was an elective member of the legislative council of New South Wales from 1848 to 1855; resigned his seat to proceed as special commissioner from New South Wales to attend the universal exhibition at Paris; received the decoration of the legion of honour; is author of a treatise on the Cultivation of Vineyards and the Art of making Wine.
MCCLINTOCK, WM. C. H.—Superintendent of river and creeks, Pomeroon, British Guiana.

events.text_span: Superintending officer, public works department, Ceylon, 1866.

raw_text: MACBRIDE, R.—Superintending officer, public works department, Ceylon, 1866.

MACDONALD, SIR JOHN ALEXANDER, K.C.B., D.C.L. (Oxon); LL.D., Q.C., Kingston, P.C.—A member of the ex-council of Canada from May 1847 to March 1848; from September 1854 to July 1858, and from August same year to May 1862, and from March 1864 until the Union, was during these periods receiver-general; from May to December, 1847, commissioner of crown lands; 1847 to March, 1848, attorney-general; from September 1854 to July 1858, when, as prime minister, he and his cabinet resigned on being defeated on the seat of government question; returned to office on 6th August same year; resigned on the following day on his re-appointment as attorney-general, which he continued to hold until the defeat of the administration on the Militia Bill in May, 1862; again attorney-general in 1864, and led the assembly from that time until the union of the British American provinces, 1867; was chairman of the London colonial conference, 1866, 1867; when the act of Union was passed by the imperial parliament on the introduction of the new constitution in July 1867, was called upon to form the first government for the new Dominion; was sworn a privy councillor, and appointed minister of justice and attorney-general of Canada; in 1871 was appointed one of H.M. joint high commissioners, together with Earl de Grey, Sir S. Northcote, Sir E. Thornton, and Mr. Bernard, for the settlement of the Alabama claims, and all matters in dispute between Great Britain and the United States, resulting in the Treaty of Washington of 1871. Eldest son of the late Hugh Macdonald, Esq., of Kingston, Ontario, and formerly of Sutherlandshire, Scotland, born in 1815; educated at the royal grammar school, Kingston, under Dr. Wilson, a Fellow of Oxford University; married (1st) Isabella, daughter of the late Alexander Clark, Esq., of Dalnavert, Invernessshire, Scotland, she died in 1850; (2nd) 1867, Susan Agnes, daughter of the late Mr. T. T. Bernard, a member of H.M. privy council of the island of Jamaica; called to the bar, in Hilary term, 1838; created queen's councillor in 1846.

MAC-DONNELL, K.C.M.G. (1871), KNIGHT BACHEL. (1855), C.B. (Civil 1852), SIR RICHARD GRAVES MAC-DONNELL.—Entered Trinity College, Dublin, 1830; gained several honours both in classics and science when an undergraduate; obtained a scholarship in 1833; graduated B.A. 1835, M.A. 1838, hon. LL.D. 1844; called to the bar in Ireland, 1838, and at Lincoln's Inn, 1840; was chief justice of the Gambia from 1843 to 1847; governor of that colony from 1847 to 1851; conducted several exploring expeditions up the Gambia, and in the interior of Africa between that river and the Senegal; appointed governor of St. Lucia, 1852, and transferred to St. Vincent same year; governor of South Australia, 1855, until March, 1862; governor of Nova Scotia, April, 1864; governor of Hong Kong, 14th Oct., 1865. Retired on pension, 1872.

McCLOSKY, JAMES HUGH, J.P. — Colonial Surgeon, Labuan. 1871; member of legislative council; entered the colonial service in 1859 late assistant-surgeon in Her Majesty's Indian army; served in the Indian campaign during mutiny in India, 1857-58 (medal).

events.position: Appointed physician to prison and police, and health officer
events.text_span: Appointed physician to prison and police, and health officer, Bahamas, 1873.

raw_text: MACCLURE, DR. W. M. G.—Appointed physician to prison and police, and health officer, Bahamas, 1873.

McCULLOCH, KNT. BACHEL. (United Kingdom, creat. 1870), K.C.M.G. (1874).—SIR JAMES McCULLOCH.—Was chief secretary to the executive government of Victoria, and in that capacity was premier of the ministry; resigned, 1872.

colonial revenue officer, June, 1871.

raw_text: McEWEN, E. JAMES.—Appointed clerk to provost marshal, Grenada, January, 1868; colonial revenue officer, June, 1871.

McEWEN, J. P.—Entered the royal navy, 1855; served in the Baltic campaign of that year in H.M.S. 'Hastings,' 60 guns, and was present at the attack on the Sandham forts at the bombardment of Helsingfors; served on the East and West Coasts of Africa in the suppression of the slave trade; was in the Niger expedition of 1864, and succeeded in ascending the Tchenchegga River in H.M.S. 'Investigator,' to within 9 miles of Bida; received a letter of thanks from the Admiralty, and one from the hydrographer, for services rendered on that occasion, and was also promoted to navigating lieutenant; was appointed to command H.M. colonial steamer 'Corra Linne,' on the West Coast of Africa, September, 1866, and afterwards to H.M. coast steamer 'Sherbro'; was appointed a commissioner under the foreign office for the settlement of the Liberian boundary question; served on the Gold Coast during a part of the Ashantee campaign, and on a mission to the Gambia in search of troops; on the return of the 'Sherbro' to England, was appointed to the command of H.M. colonial steamer 'Pluto,' in the Straits Settlement; resigned, April, 1875; assistant harbour-master, Hong Kong.

clerk in the registry department, Mar. 1864
clerk in the general department, July, 1870.

raw_text: MAC GEE, W. J.—Extra clerk, colonial office, April, 1863; clerk in the registry department, Mar. 1864; clerk in the general department, July, 1870.

MACKGLASHAN, JOHN CHAS.—Chief clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, Jamaica; is also clerk of the privy council; was appointed clerk in executive committee office, in June, 1856; was, in April, 1866, on the death of the secretary to the executive committee and clerk, privy council, appointed provisionally to those offices, pending the change in the constitution of the island, under which it became a crown colony; held those appointments until the inauguration of the new form of government; then appointed chief clerk in the financial secretary's office; and in the amalgamation of that office in February, 1870, with the colonial secretariat, appointed chief clerk in the latter office; acted as assistant colonial secretary from April to October, 1870, and again from June to December in 1871; acting auditor-general from September 1872; to January 1873; acting assistant colonial secretary, January, 1873; acting colonial secretary, February, 1873; acting assistant, June, 1873; acting colonial secretary, 28th August, 1873; January, 1874; and acting assistant, July, 1874; auditor-general, 1875.

McDOUGALL, W., C.B.—Born 1822; admitted as an attorney of Upper Canada, 1847; called to the bar, 1862; for many years on the daily political press of Toronto; sat in Canadian parliament from 1858 up to 1872; is now a member of the Ontario assembly; appointed commissioner of crown lands, 24th May, 1862; provincial secretary, 31st June, 1864; minister of public works of the dominion, 1st July, 1867; lieut.-governor of Rupert's land and the north-west territories, October, 1869; was a special commissioner to England on several occasions, most notably in connection with confederation, the acquisition of the north-west territories, immigration, and on the subject of defence.

events.text_span: Appointed clerk in the surveyor-general's office, Natal, 1845

raw_text: M'GILL, E. F.—Appointed clerk in the surveyor-general's office, Natal, 1845, and clerk and draughtsman in the civil engineers' department of that colony, 1860; chief clerk and accountant, 1865.
MCHATTIE, A. G., M.D., M.R.C.S., ENGLAND.—Medical officer district No. 2, Antigua.

MACKENZIE, HON. ALEXANDER.—Born near Dunkeld, Perthshire, 28th January, 1822. Educated at the public schools of Moulin, Dunkeld, and Perth. Married, 1st, Helen, daughter of the late Mr. Wm. Neil, of Irvine, Scotland (she died 2nd January, 1852); 2ndly, 17th June, 1853, Jane, eldest daughter of the late Mr. Robert Sym, of Perthshire, Scotland. In youth learned the business of a builder and architect, and after arrival in Canada, became a contractor. For some years edited the 'Lambton Shield,' a reform journal. Is president of the Isolated risk and fire insurance company; and a commissioner ex officio to assist Mr. Speaker in the management of the interior economy of the house of commons. Was major 27th Lambton battalion of volunteer infantry, for some years up to October, 1874, when he resigned. Was a member of executive council, and treasurer of Ontario (in Mr. Blake's administration), from 21st December, 1871, until October, 1872, when he retired with Mr. Blake for the purpose of devoting themselves entirely to Dominion politics. Declined a seat in the Canadian cabinet, on the occasion of the retirement of Mr. Brown, 1865. Was leader of Ontario reform opposition in house of commons from the union until 1873, when chosen leader of the whole opposition party, and continued in that position until 5th November, 1873, when, owing to the resignation of Sir John Macdonald, he was called upon to form a new administration, a duty he succeeded in accomplishing on 7th of the same month, when he and his colleagues were sworn of the privy council, he (Mr. Mackenzie) taking the office of minister of public works. Proceeded to the mother country on public business, June, 1875, and while there was presented with the freedom of Irvine, Dundee, and Perth. Sat for Lambton in Canada assembly from 1861 until the union. Returned to house of commons at general election, 1867; and at general election, 1872, re-elected by acclamation on his appointment to office, and again at last general election. Represented West Middlesex in Ontario assembly from general election, 1871, until October, 1872, when he resigned. Several important public measures owe their existence to Mr. Mackenzie. as a private member, viz.: the act amending the assessment act of Upper Canada (1863); that consolidating and amending the acts relating to the assessment of property, Upper Canada (1866); and the highly useful measure for providing means of egress from public buildings (1866). As chairman of committee on municipal and assessment laws (1866), wrote and framed the greater part of the general act on municipal corporations, &c. All the measures of his government, including the enactment of a stringent election law, with the trial of election petitions by judges, and vote by ballot; the abolition of the real estate qualification of members; the inspection of produce and weights and measures; the better administration of penitentiaries; the enactment of the marine telegraph law, which virtually abolishes the monopoly of the cable company; the establishment of a Dominion military college, and the improvement of the militia system; the enlargement of the canals; the permanent organization of the civil service; the establishment of a supreme court for the Dominion; the reduction of postage to and from the United States; the free delivery of postal matter in cities and towns; the opening of direct mail communication with the West Indies; the construction of a transcontinental telegraph line; the better administration of Government railways; an improved copyright law; a new insolvent law; and the establishment of a territorial government for the great North-West, have all been more or less his work. In addition, two very important questions, which for some time agitated the public mind and threatened the gravest complications— the Manitoba amnesty and the New Brunswick school questions—have been satisfactorily adjusted during Mr. Mackenzie's administration.
MACKENZIE, REV. D. C.—Chaplain, Kandian District, Ceylon.

MCKINNEY, W. J.—Postmaster of British Honduras; appointed 1859; is secretary to the council for education; served as an ensign in the Belize volunteers, under Captain Edmunds, late 4th W.L. regt. on the northern frontier of the colony in 1866-67, against the hostile Indians of Yucatan; was mentioned in despatches, and received the thanks of the legislative assembly for his services acting police magistrate, 1875.

raw_text: MCKINSTRY, T. W.—Chief Clerk, colonial secretary's office, Barbados, appointed 1872; served many years in the commissariat department in British North America and in the West Indian islands; retired from the army as assistant commissary-general in 1870, receiving the commutation allowance.

MACLEAN, C. R.—Acting stipendiary magistrate of the 4th judicial district, from 20th June, 1862, to 23rd June, 1863, St. Lucia. In Dec., 1863, again acted as special justice of the 4th district, in which appointment he was afterwards confirmed; in Nov., 1867, removed from the 4th to the 1st judicial district; performed also the duties of coroner, visiting justice of the Royal gaol, and district road commissioner, until the latter office was abolished in 1871; in March, 1868, was appointed official member of the legislative council, and in March, 1871, appointed chairman of the poor law committee of the 1st district.

MACLEAN, SIR DONALD, K.C.M.G. (1874).—Minister for native affairs, New Zealand; appointed to be a companion of the Order of St. Michael and St. George in 1870, and promoted to be a Knight commander of that Order in 1874.
MACLEAR, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1860.)—SIR THOS.—Astronomer-royal at the Cape.

MACLEAY, SIR GEORGE, K.C.M.G. (1875), (C.M.G. 1869).—Eldest surviving son of the late Alexander Macleay, Esq., for many years secretary to the transport board, and subsequently colonial secretary and first speaker of the legislative assembly of New South Wales; was educated at Westminster; went at an early age to New South Wales; accompanied in 1829-30, Captain Sturt on his expedition down the river Murrumbidgee and Murray, and was for several years a member of the legislature of New South Wales.

MC NAIR, J. F. A., MAJOR, R.A. (late Madras).—Is a cadet of 1845; served with his battery in India until 1850, and in the Straits Settlements, and Labuan from 1853 to 1857; appointed private secretary to the governor Straits Settlements, and subsequently executive engineer and superintendent of convicts; returned to Europe in 1865; employed under surveyor-general of prisons in the erection of the Woking female prison; appointed chief engineer and controller of convicts, Straits Settlements, on their transfer to the crown in 1867; acted as colonial secretary in 1868; appointed surveyor-general, 1872; is a member of both executive and legislative councils; Associate Institution of Civil Engineers, F.L.S., F.R.G.S., F.A.S.

MCNEILL, COLONEL JOHN CARSTAIRS, C.B., (1874), C.M.G. (1870), V.C.—Served during the campaign of 1857-58 in India as aide-de-camp to Sir F. Lugard during the siege and capture of Lucknow; in 1861, proceeded with Sir D. Cameron to New Zealand as his aide-de-camp, and served there until 1865; commanded the Tipperary flying column during the Fenian disturbances in the winter of 1866-67; served on the staff of the Red River expedition from Canada under Sir Garnet Wolseley, in 1870, and again under that officer in the Ashanti war, 1873-4; appointed an equerry in ordinary to Her Majesty, 1 Aug., 1874.

MAGALHAENS, JOZE MARIA RODRIGUES.—Appointed in 1848 as overseer to superintend the preparing of the granite stones for the construction of the Horsburgh Lighthouse; in 1850 was transferred to convict department up to 1854, during which time had charge of the House of Correction, superintended all the works both civil and military, and was transferred again to the Horsburgh Lighthouse; about 1854 was recalled and promoted to be head overseer, had charge of the town and country roads divisions; assisted to superintend all the military works in town, Mount Faber, and Mount Palmer, and was posted at the Rabbit and Coney, during the construction of the Raffles Lighthouse; about 1858 was promoted again to be assistant supervisor, and ordered to take charge of the works during the construction of the Field Fortress, Port Canning; after completion, returned to the town division and assisted in preparing the granite courses for the Alguada Lighthouse; in 1863 was transferred to Penang to take charge of Province Wellesley, and after eight months, recalled to take charge of the Singapore town division; in 1864 was promoted to be supervisor, in 1866 transferred to take charge of the Penang Division of Province Wellesley; in 1867 promoted to be assistant engineer.

MAIS, C. HENRY.—Appointed engineer-in-chief, South Australia, 1867. In addition to above, general manager of railways, 1867 to June, 1869; manager of railways from June, 1870.

MALCOLM, WILLIAM ROLLE.—Educated at Eton and Balliol College, Oxford; first class moderations, 1860, first class final classical examination, 1862; elected fellow of All Souls, 1864; called to the bar, L.I., 1865; secretary public schools commission, 1868; assistant secretary Board of Trade, 1870; appointed an assistant under secretary colonial office, 24 July, 1874.

MANN, MAJOR-GENERAL J. R., R.E.—Director of roads and surveyor-general of Jamaica; entered the corps of royal engineers on the 18th of Dec. 1840; surveyor-general of Mauritius from 23rd Dec., 1856, to 3rd May, 1861; appointed director of roads and surveyor-general of Jamaica, January, 1867; resigned 1873, and re-appointed in Feb., 1874; appointed an official member of the legislative council, Feb., 1874.

MANNING, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.)—SIR WILLIAM MONTAGU MANNING, L.L.D.—Educated at Tavistock, at Southampton, and at University College, London; called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1832; was chairman of quarter sessions in New South Wales from Oct., 1837 to Oct., 1844; solicitor-general from Oct. 1844 to Jan. 1848; acting judge of the supreme court from Jan. 1848 to Nov. 1849; again solicitor-general from Nov. 1849 to May, 1856; attorney-general from May, 1856, to May, 1858; is a member of the executive council of New South Wales, and one of the queen's counsel there; author of 'Notes of Proceedings in Electoral Revision Courts,' 'Nevile and Manning's Reports in Court of King's Bench,' 1832 to 1837.

raw_text: MARSHALL, JAMES.—Called to the bar, 1868; went the northern circuit; appointed chief magistrate of the Gold Coast, 1873; rendered valuable service during the Ashantee war, for which he has been granted the medal; appointed puisne judge of the supreme court of the Gold Coast Colony, 1876.

MARTIN, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1869.)—Q.C., SIR JAMES MARTIN. Born 1820; educated at Sydney College, New South Wales; was called to the bar of New South Wales 1856 (having been for some time previously an attorney of the supreme court); appointed a queen's counsel there in 1857. Was five times attorney-general of New South Wales between 1856 and 1872, during four years of which time he filled the office of premier. Elected a member of the Senate of Sydney University Aug., 1858. Appointed chief justice of New South Wales, 1873.

events.text_span: appointed 1860.

raw_text: MARTIN, HENRY D.—Harbour-master, Grenada; appointed 1860.

raw_text: MARTINDALE, FELIX.—Was clerk in the Mauritius currency bank, June, 1848; clerk to marine court, Feb. 1849; clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Plaines Wilhems, March, 1850; district clerk, Moka, May, 1862.

MASON, J. D.—Educated at "The College," Llandovery, and the London University; appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 1872; acting police magistrate, Panadura, Sep. 1872; acting assistant government agent, Galle, Nov. 1872; police magistrate, Balapitimodara, June, 1873, to continue to act at Galle.

MASSIE, ROBERT.—Appointed a writer in the Ceylon civil service, 1865; acting assistant government agent, Trincomalee, Jan. 1866; police magistrate, Kalpitya, Feb. 1867; assistant collector and landing surveyor, Jaffna, Oct. 1867; has acted as assistant government agent at Kuruegalla, Jaffna, Mullaitivu, Colombo and Matala, police magistrate, Galle, Oct. 1872, acting at Matala also.

MATHEWS, THOMAS MARTIN.—Entered the Bahamas service as clerk in the police office, and held that appointment for upwards of twelve years; was admitted to the bar in Easter term, 1833; held for many years the appointment of assistant clerk to the house of assembly, and afterwards that of chief clerk was appointed a lieutenant of militia in 1834, and in the year 1836 became military secretary to the commander-in-chief; was a member of the house of assembly for many years; was appointed judge advocate of militia in the year 1840; held the appointment of acting attorney-general in 1850; and in the same year was appointed member of the executive council. In 1852 was appointed on two occasions judge of the general court; in 1853, again acted as attorney-general and advocate and procurator-general in admiralty; again held those appointments in the years 1856 and 1860; held the appointment of acting judge of the court of common pleas on three occasions in the years 1874 and 1875; in the latter year was appointed queen's counsel and acting chief justice; legislative councillor, 1876.

MAURITIUS, 4th BISHOP of, PETER SORENSON, ROYSTON.—Trinity College, Cambridge, B.A. in classical and mathematical honours, 1853; M.A., 1860; D.D., 1872; resident tutor of Church Missionary College, 1853-55; corresponding secretary of South India mission, 1855-71, except from 1864-66, when incumbent of Plaines Wilhems, Mauritius; consecrated 1872.

MAXSE, LIEUT.-COLONEL H. F. BERKELEY, C.M.G. (1874).—Appointed lieut.-governor of Heliogoland in 1863; and governor on the 6th Feb. 1864; entered the army in 1849, and served in the Eastern campaign of 1854 as aide-de-camp to Lord Cardigan, including the battles of Alma and Balaclava (wounded), siege of Sebastopol; medal and clasps, 5th class of the Medjidié and Turkish medals.

MAXWELL, KNT. BACH. (Creat. 1856).—SIR PETER BENSON MAXWELL. Son of the rev. Peter Benson Maxwell, of Birdstown, co. Donegal; born 1815; married, 1842, daughter of Francis Synge, Esq., of Glanmore castle, co. Wicklow; educated in Paris, and at Trinity College, Dublin; was called to the bar of the Middle Temple, 1841, and went the home circuit; appointed recorder of Penang, Straits Settlements, February, 1856 appointed recorder of Singapore, June, 1866. Retired 1871.

events.text_span: Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana, August, 1869.

raw_text: MAXWELL, PETER BENSON. — Stipendiary magistrate, British Guiana, August, 1869.

MAXWELL, ROBERT WALTER.—Was clerk to the chief justice of the Straits Settlements from January, 1869, till February, 1871; during the absence of the chief justice in Europe in 1867-1868 acted as secretary to the lieutenant-governor at Penang; acting superintendent of police and justice of the peace, Singapore in 1871 and 1872; was appointed superintendent of police Straits Settlements in December, 1873.

MAXWELL, WILLIAM EDWARD.—Entered the service as clerk to the recorder of Penang, Jan. 1, 1865; appointed acting senior sworn clerk to the registrar of the court of judicature, Penang division, March, 1865; confirmed in the same appointment, September, 1866; passed as a law-agent of the local bar, January, 1868; acting registrar, supreme court, Penang, November, 1868; senior sworn clerk, supreme court, Singapore, February, 1869; police magistrate, and commissioner of the court of requests, Penang, September, 1869; transferred in the same capacities to Malacca, February, 1870; administered the government at Malacca, in Aug. and Sept. in the same year during the absence of the lieutenant-governor; transferred to Singapore, August, 1871, and acted as police magistrate there until April, 1872, gazetted as police magistrate and commissioner of the court of requests, province Wellesley, April, 1872. Admitted a student at the Inner Temple, Nov. 1872; passed the general examination at Lincoln's Inn, May, 1837, and went the Oxford and Midland Circuits in the same year as marshal to Mr. Justice Honyman. Resumed charge in province Wellesley, Dec., 1873; temporary judge of the supreme court, Penang, May, 1874. Assistant government agent, province Wellesley, Sep. 1874.

raw_text: MAY, CHARLES. — Superintendent of police, Hong Kong; appointed 1844; promoted first police magistrate, 1862; sheriff of Hong Kong, Jan. 1864; acting treasurer, Feb. 1872.

raw_text: MAYER, G. C. — Acting district magistrate, Seychelles, Jan. 1865; district magistrate, Mauritius, Oct., 1865.

raw_text: MAYNE, ROBERT DAWSON. — Appointed chief magistrate, Lagos, 1872; stipendiary magistrate, Trinidad, 1874.

MAYO, CHARLES, M.D. — Government medical officer for Vanua Levu and the Eastern Islands, Fiji, appointed Feb., 1875; educated at Winchester and New College, Oxford; fellow of New College, B.A., 1859; M.A., 1862; M.B., 1865; M.D., 1871; formerly house physician to St. Bartholomew's Hospital; M.R.C.S., 1861; M.R.C.P., 1869; volunteered for medical service in the American war, 1862-3 (staff surgeon-major); was director of the Alice Hospital at Darmstadt in the Franco-German war, 1870-1; and served with the Dutch expedition to Atchin, 1873-4, with the same rank in both cases; late senior physician to the Royal General Dispensary, London.

MEADE, HONORABLE ROBERT HENRY. — Is an M.A. of Exeter College, Oxford; passed an examination before the civil service commissioners, May 9th, 1859; was appointed a junior clerk in the foreign office, June 1st, 1859; was attached to Lord Dufferin's special mission to Syria, from July 31, 1860, till Sept. 1861; accompanied the Prince of Wales during His Royal Highness's tour in the East, Germany, and Italy, in 1862; accompanied Earl Russell to Germany when his lordship was in attendance on Her Majesty, in the autumn of 1862; and accompanied Earl Granville to Germany on a similar occasion, in 1863, and again in 1865; was appointed groom of the bedchamber to H.R.H. the Prince of Wales, Nov. 27th, 1862; and was private secretary to Earl Granville, from June, 1864, till July, 1866; private secretary to Lord Granville when secretary of state for the colonies, from 9th Dec. 1868, till July, 1870, and when secretary of state for foreign affairs, 1870; assistant under secretary, colonial office, May, 1871.

raw_text: MEADE, JAMES. —Acted as puisne justice, Montserrat, in 1852; appointed a justice of peace and provost-marshal in 1853; a member of the executive and legislative councils in 1861; promoted to the offices of colonial secretary and treasurer in March, 1865; appointed a member of the legislative council, 1867; is registrar of deeds; registrar of the courts; comptroller of customs; and registrar of shipping.

raw_text: MEERES, REV. H. W. —Colonial chaplain, Trinity Church, Colombo, Ceylon, 1872.

MEHEUX, J. —Was appointed ensign and paymaster of the Sierra Leone Militia in 1840; went same year on an expedition to Cobolo; promoted lieutenant, 1842, and captain 1846; appointed clerk of the crown and recorder, 1847; acting marshal of the Vice-Admiralty Court, 1848; proctor, 1848; postmaster, 1848; admitted attorney and notary public, 1855; sheriff and provost-marshal, 1855; official assignee and registrar of the Insolvent Court, 1864; marshal Vice-Admiralty Court, 1865; acting colonial treasurer, from 24th October to 22nd November, 1873; acting inspector general of police, from 12th December, 1873, to 4th January, 1874; acting colonial treasurer, jointly with the auditor general, from 1st October to 31st December, 1874; acting collector of customs, from 12th December, 1873, to 31st May, 1875. MELBOURNE (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP OF. RIGHT REV. JAMES MOORHOUSE, D.D. —Formerly Vicar of Paddington, London; consecrated 22nd October, 1876.

raw_text: MELDRUM, C. —Professor of mathematics, Royal College, Mauritius, 1861; director of the Port Louis Observatory, 1873; director of the Royal Alfred Observatory.

MELVILLE, GEORGE. —Late lieutenant, Kent Artillery Militia; entered the imperial civil service in June, 1862, and continued in that service until 25th November, 1874, when he was appointed first clerk and cashier of the treasury, Sierra Leone; acted as colonial treasurer from the 13th December, 1874, to the 12th February, 1876, during which period also acted as colonial secretary from the 17th July to the 17th September, 1876, and as coroner in April, 1875, and from 7th July to November, of the same year, and acted as collector of customs from the 19th June to the 12th September, 1876, and as colonial surveyor, from the 27th July to the 15th September, 1876; holds commissions as deputy coroner and justice of the peace.

raw_text: MENZIES, W., LIEUT. R.M. —Appointed inspector of police, Antigua, 1870; inspector-general of Leeward Island police, 1874.

MEREDITH, EDMUND A., LL.D. —Was provincial secretary for Upper Canada from 1847 until 1st July, 1867, when appointed under secretary of state for the province; appointed deputy of the minister of the interior, 1st July, 1873; has been chairman of the board of prison inspectors; and now is chairman of the civil service board.

MERILMAN, T. R. —Appointed clerk to the senior judge of the court of the eastern districts of the Cape Colony, in February, 1865. 2nd class clerk in the Cape Civil Service, August, 1869; transferred to Griqualand West, as clerk to the recorder, January, 1872. Appointed chief clerk in the office of the secretary to government, Griqualand West, March, 1873.

MESSERVY, CHARLES. —Educated at Victoria College, Jersey; appointed assistant colonial engineer, Lagos, November, 1870; assistant to superintendent of public works, Trinidad, Nov. 1871; acting superintendent of public works, November, 1873, to November, 1874.

raw_text: METZGER, JOSEPH M. —Educated at the Freetown Grammar School, and the Fourah Bay College, Sierra Leone; entered the civil service as junior clerk in the police office, Freetown, August, 1865; acted as clerk in February, 1866; became clerk of police in April, 1866; transferred to the colonial secretary's office as second clerk in June, 1875.

MILLET, J. C. —Employed as assistant clerk in colonial treasury department, Trinidad, 1839; copyist in registrar general's department, 1858, registrar of births, deaths, &c., Feb. 1859; registrar of vaccination, April, 1864.

MILLNER, JAMES STOKES. —Educated at Marischal College, University of Aberdeen; licentiate of faculty of physicians and surgeons, and licentiate in midwifery, Glasgow. Settled in South Australia, 1855; appointed justice of the peace and coroner, 1861; medical officer and protector of aborigines in the Northern Territory of South Australia, 1865; returned to Adelaide on the recall of the party, 1867; appointed acting government resident, special magistrate, medical officer, and protector of aborigines on the re-establishment of the Northern Territory, 1869, which offices, except the first, he now holds.
MILNE, SIR WILLIAM, KNT. BACHEL. (1876). —President of the legislative council of South Australia.

raw_text: MISSO, M. B. —Appointed medical sub-assistant, Ceylon, 1837; 3rd class, 1842; 2nd class, 1847; medical assistant, 1858; assist. colonial surgeon, 1867.

MITCHELL, CHARLES A. W.—Third clerk in the immigration office, Trinidad, 1859; 2nd clerk, 1868; chief clerk, 1864; in 1867 magistrate and warden to Toco ward union; in 1870, appointed, in conjunction with Sir George Young and W. E. Frere, Esq., commissioner to inquire into the treatment of Indian immigrants in Demerara; superintendent of crown lands, Fiji, 1875.

raw_text: MITCHELL, CAPTAIN C. B. H., R.M. (seconded.) —Colonial secretary of British Honduras, July 29, 1868.
MITCHELL, H.—Agent-general of immigrants, Trinidad; appointed 1853. Was assistant-superintendent from 1852 to 1853.

raw_text: MITCHELL, REV. H. L.—Appointed Scotch chaplain, Kandyarr province, Ceylon, 1862; and colonial chaplain Presbyterian church, Galle, 1870.

MITCHELL, P.—Born 1824, called to the bar of New Brunswick, 1848; sat in New Brunswick legislative assembly from 1856 to 1860, when appointed to the legislative council; in 1867, called to the senate of the dominion, where he remained until 1872, when he resigned and was elected to the house of commons; a member of the executive council of New Brunswick from 1858 to 1865, and from April, 1866 to the union in 1867 (in his own administration); was minister of marine and fisheries for the dominion from 1st July, 1867, until 5th November, 1873.

MITCHELL, ROBERT W. S.—Appointed in 1867 warden of the district of Montserrat, in the island of Trinidad, and commissioner of crown lands; in 1869 appointed sub-intendant of crown lands, charged with the general management of all crown lands in the colony; justice of the peace for the county of Caroni, 1868; for the counties of St. George and St. Patrick, 1870. Acted as assistant protector of immigrants in Mauritius, in 1872, and as agent general of immigrants in Trinidad in 1873; appointed immigration agent for Trinidad, at Calcutta, 1873.
MITCHELL, SAMUEL.—Colonial secretary, registrar, and clerk of the executive council, Grenada; appointed 1857. Entered the colonial service, 1845; was chief clerk, colonial secretary's office, 1854.
MITCHELL, SIR WILLIAM HENRY FANCOURT, Knight Bach. (1875), —President of the Legislative Council, Victoria.

events.text_span: Bishop Barbados, 1873

raw_text: MITCHINSON, RIGHT REV. JOHN, D.C.L.—Bishop Barbados, 1873; was head master, King's School, Canterbury.

events.position: Appointed assistant clerk, colonial secretary's office
events.text_span: Appointed assistant clerk, colonial secretary's office, Dominica, 1868
clerk, president's office, 1872.

raw_text: MODESTE, WILLIAM.—Appointed assistant clerk, colonial secretary's office, Dominica, 1868; clerk, president's office, 1872.

MOIR, ALEXANDER WILSON.—Entered the service in the customs department in Jamaica in Feb. 1843; appointed presiding magistrate of the (late) colony of Bay Islands in Dec. 1855; public treasurer of Honduras, July, 1860, but at the urgent request of the Spanish Honduras government, was continued at his post in the Bay Islands, until the cession of the colony to the republic on the 1st June, 1861; acting colonial secretary with a dormant commission as superintendent at the same place in June, 1862; president of the Turks and Caicos Island in Aug. 1862; president of the Virgin Islands, Oct., 1869; president of Dominica, June, 1871; president of St. Kitts, Feb. 1872; a member of the executive council of the Leeward Islands (1871), and a nominated member of the general council.

raw_text: MOIR, R. W. D.—Was appointed acting assistant government agent, commissioner of requests, and police magistrate, at Mulaitivu, Ceylon, 1856; commissioner of requests at Calpentyn, 1861; assistant government agent at Mulaitivu, 1861; acting landing surveyor, Colombo, April, 1865; appointment confirmed, September, 1865; acting collector of customs, Galle, 1866; assistant government agent and district judge, &c., Trincomalee, 1870.

MOLLAN, LT.-COL. WILLIAM CAMPBELL, C.B.—Served in the Indian campaign 1857-59; commanded the 75th regiment with Outram's force at Alumbagh from Dec. 1857 to Feb. 1858, including repulse of attacks on 11th and 16th of January, and minor affairs; served as brigade major with the rifle brigade at the siege and capture of Lucknow, and actions of Koorsee, Baree, Simree, Nawabgunge (horse killed by round shot), and Sultampore, operations terminating in the passage of the Goontee on 28th August; action at Doodpore and capture of the enemy's guns, surrender of the forts of Amethie and Shankapore (frequently mentioned in despatches, medal with clasp); is a graduate of the Dublin University, and a justice of the peace for the county Kilkenny; a district magistrate of St. Vincent, 1873; police magistrate, Bahamas, 1874; police magistrate, Gibraltar, 1875.

MOLONEY, CAPTAIN C. ALFRED.—Entered the military college, Sandhurst, February, 1866; ensign, February, 1867; lieutenant, December, 1858; captain, November, 1874. Acted as civil commandant of the Quiah district, Sierra Leone, 1867-8; aide-de-camp to Sir James Walker, C.B., governor of the Bahamas, Feb., 1871; private secretary to Capt. G. C. Strahan, administrator of the government of the Bahamas, from April, 1871, to May, 1873; acted in the same capacity under administrator J. S. Dumaresq; private secretary and aide-de-camp to governor Hennessy, C.M.G., from June, 1873, to 10th November, 1873, when he embarked for service on the Gold Coast. Served in the Ashantee campaign, 1873-4 (medal); private secretary and aide-de-camp to governor Strahan, C.M.G., July, 1874. Acted as paymaster, Gold Coast constabulary, from July, 1874, to February, 1875. Commanded the troops on the Gold Coast on the occasion of the abolition of slavery, 4th November, 1874 (mentioned in the governor's despatches). Member of executive and legislative councils of the Gold Coast colony, 8th January, 1875. Appointed acting auditor of the Gold Coast colony, 1st February, 1875. Took over command as acting inspector-general of the constabulary of the Gold Coast colony, 29th March, 1875.

MONCK, 4th VISCOUNT (Creat. 1800); BARON MONCK, 1797 (Ire.); BARON MONCK (United Kingdom), 1866, by which title he holds his seat in the House of Lords; privy councillor, 1869; G.C.M.G., 1869.—CHARLES STANLEY MONCK.—Succeeded his father in 1849; educated at Trinity College, Dublin; called to the bar in Ireland, 1841; was a lord of the treasury from 1855 to 1858; is a deputy-lieut. of Wicklow; was an unsuccessful candidate at the Wicklow election, May, 1848; was M.P. for Portsmouth from 1852 to 1857; was appointed a commissioner of charitable donations and bequests (Ire.) 1851; governor-general of Canada, Oct. 1861, and reappointed in 1867 governor-general of the dominion of Canada. Resigned 1868; commissioner of national education in Ireland, Dec. 1871; one of the lords justices, Ireland, 1873.

MONTREAL, 3rd BISHOP of. Founded 1836.— RIGHT REV. ASHTON OXENDEN, D.D.—Educated at University College, Oxford, where he graduated B.A. 1831; ordained deacon, 1833; priest, 1834; appointed rector of Pluckley-with-Povington, Ashford, Kent, in 1848; was proctor in convocation for the diocese of Canterbury; consecrated 3rd Bishop of Montreal, 1869, in succession to the late Bishop Fulford; is also metropolitan of Canada. This diocese is situated in Lower Canada, and was formerly a portion of the diocese of Quebec. Annual value 800 l. from the colonial bishoprics' fund, &c.

MOODIE, W. J. D.—Was employed in the office of the colonial secretary of the Cape of Good Hope, 1843; in that of the colonial secretary of Natal from 1846 to 1859, in which he became chief clerk; was appointed resident magistrate.

MOODY, HARRY, M.A., and late fellow of King's College, Cambridge, and Captain in the Oxfordshire militia.—A.D.C. and private secretary to lieut.-governor of New Brunswick, Oct. 1861, to March, 1867; private secretary to governor of Trinidad, and acting auditor-general of that colony, April, 1867; resigned on account of ill-health, July, 1867; private secretary to lieut.-governor of Nova Scotia, Oct. 1867; was secretary to governor-general of Canada, March to Oct., 1875.

MOORE, W. D. F.—Appointed deputy inspector of police, Singapore, 20th October, 1865; joined the office of commissioner of police, Straits Settlements, as chief clerk on 1st February, 1868; appointed chief clerk to magistrate of police, Singapore, 4th August, 1869; and on 18th August, 1871, appointed acting chief clerk to the lieut.-governor, Penang; promoted to be chief clerk Colonial Secretary's office, Singapore, on the 1st September, 1872; senior sworn clerk, supreme court, 15th August, 1875.

raw_text: MORGAN, HENRY JAMES.—Entered Canadian civil service, 1853; called to the bar of the province of Quebec, 1873; to that of Ontario same year; appointed keeper of state records for the dominion, Oct., 1873; promoted to be a chief clerk in the civil service, Dec., 1875; is a commissioner for taking affidavits in all the courts in the provinces of Ontario, Quebec, Nova Scotia, and Manitoba. Author of "The Tour of H.R.H. the Prince of Wales through British America and the United States" (1860); "Sketches of Celebrated Canadians and persons connected with Canada" (1862); "Bibliotheca Canadensis, or a Manual of Canadian Literature" (1837); and is editor of the "Canadian Parliamentary Companion," an annual publication, first established in 1862.

MORGAN, JOHN.—Assistant harbour-master, Port Louis, Mauritius; appointed March, 1860. Entered the colonial service in October, 1852, as mate of the colonial steamer; was promoted to the office of pilot in March, 1854.

raw_text: MOSELEY, FRANCIS ARNOLD.—Is a member of the colonial bar; entered the public service as acting clerk in the colonial secretary's department, Nassau, Bahamas, in April, 1872; appointed private secretary to Governor Pope Hennessy, November, 1873.

MOSELEY, HENRY EDWARD.—Was second clerk in the receiver-general and treasurer's department, Bahamas, from 8th August, 1863, to 30th June, 1865; appointed librarian to the Nassau public library and clerk to the board of public works, 1st July, 1865; acting clerk in the colonial secretary's office, 17th April, 1871; acted as registrar of records from 1st April to 19th November, 1872; appointed clerk, colonial secretary's department, 1st April, 1873.

MOSS, THOMAS.—Born, 1836; called to the bar of Upper Canada in Michaelmas term, 1861; created a queen's counsel, 1872; a member of the law reform commission of Ontario, 1872; declined the vice-chancellorship of Ontario, 1873; sat in house of commons from Nov., 1873, until Oct., 1875, when appointed a puisne judge of the court of error and appeal of Ontario; is vice-chancellor of Toronto university.

MOSSE, CHARLES BENJAMIN, C.B. (1874), A.M., T.C.D., M.R.C.S., L.R. and Q.C., Ph. and L.M., F.R.Z.S., Deputy Surgeon-General, A.M.D.—Served in medical charge of expeditionary force up the river Gambia, West Africa, June, 1866; present at the assault and capture of the stockaded Maudingo town of Tubarcolong (mentioned in despatches); specially promoted staff surgeon (1867) for "valuable services" during epidemic of yellow fever at Bathurst, Gambia; held the acting appointments of queen's advocate (member of council), chief magistrate, colonial surgeon, and inspector of prisons, when serving at Gambia and on the Gold Coast, West Africa; served throughout the Ashantee war, 1873-74; present at the action of Essaman (mentioned in despatches); also at the battles of Amoaful and Ordahsu, and the capture of Coomassie (mentioned in despatches, C.B., and medal with clasp); appointed, June, 1876, superintending medical officer for Jamaica.

MULDER, J. H.—Registrar of Springfield cemetery, St. Kitts, 16th March, 1869; clerk to colonial secretary, Nevis, from March, 1869, to March, 1872; assistant clerk in president's office, St. Kitts, April, 1872.

events.text_span: Appointed government medical officer, Jamaica, 1873.

raw_text: MULLEN, J. J.—Appointed government medical officer, Jamaica, 1873.

MUNDY, ROBERT MILLER, C.M.G. (1874)—Cadet Royal military academy, Woolwich, Feb. 1828; lieutenant royal artillery, June, 1833; lieutenant royal horse artillery, March, 1841, to April, 1844; 2nd captain, 1844; brevet major, Oct. 1846; invalided on half-pay, Oct. 1847; appointed magistrate for county of Hants, June, 1851; served as lieutenant-colonel Osmanli horse artillery, in Turkey, March, 1855, to Aug. 1856; is lieut.-colonel in the Turkish service, and has received 3rd class Medjidie; appointed lieutenant-governor of Grenada, Sept. 1863; administered the general government of the Windward Islands, April to Dec. 1865; administered government of British Guiana, May, 1866 to Sept. 1867; an expression of Her Majesty's approbation of his services, conveyed by the secretary of state in despatch of 6th Nov. 1867; administered general government of Windward Islands, from June, 1868, to April, 1869; and selected to administer the general government of the Leeward Isles during Sir B. Pine's leave of absence, 1871. Appointed lieutenant-governor, British Honduras, Feb. 1874.

raw_text: MUSGRAVE, SIR ANTHONY, K.C.M.G. (1875), (C.M.G. 1871).—Was private secretary to Mr. Mackintosh when governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, 1850-51; entered as student at the Inner Temple, 1851; appointed treasury accountant at Antigua, 1852; resumed legal studies at the Temple in 1853; appointed colonial secretary of Antigua, Feb. 1854; administrator of the colony of Nevis, Oct. 1860; administrator of government of St. Vincent, April, 1861; appointed lieut.-governor of St. Vincent, May, 1862; governor of Newfoundland, April, 1864; governor of British Columbia, June 1869; lieutenant-governor of Natal, May, 1872; governor South Australia, 1873; governor of Jamaica, 1877.

MUSGRAVE, ANTHONY, (Junr.)—Appointed Private Secretary to Governor Musgrave, in Newfoundland, from July, 1868, to July, 1869. Served in same capacity in British Columbia from August, 1869 to April, 1872. Private secretary and clerk of executive council in Natal, July, 1872 to 1873. Appointed private secretary and clerk of executive council in South Australia, June, 1873.

MUSSON, EDWARD H., St. Vincent.—Immigration agent and clerk of executive council and administrative committee, 1859; ex officio auditor of public accounts, 1863; auditor of public accounts, permanent establishment, 1864; selected ex officio member of the legislative assembly from its inauguration, January, 1868, to its abolition at end of 1875; member of H. M. Executive Council, July, 1868; resigned July, 1875; captain and garrison adjutant of militia, 1863.

MUSSON, SAMUEL PAYNTER.—Appointed a clerk in the governor's private secretary's office, Jamaica, 1st April, 1866. On change of constitution, appointed a second-class clerk in the office of the financial secretary, 16th September, 1866; promoted to be a first class clerk in the colonial secretariat, Jamaica, 1st October, 1872.

NAPIER OF MAGDALA, 1st BARON (Great Britain), created, 1868.—G.C.B., 1868; G.C.S.I., 1867.—ROBERT CORNELIUS NAPIER.—Educated at the Royal Military College at Addiscombe; entered the Bengal engineers, 1826; was brigade-major in the Sutlej campaigns of 1845-6, where he was severely wounded; was acting chief engineer during part of the siege of Moultan, where he was again wounded; promoted to lieut.-colonel for his services at Goojerat, 1849; commanded the engineers during the operations which ended in the capture of Lucknow, 1858; commanded a brigade at the capture of Gwalior same year, and defeated a large body of rebels at the battle of Pawsee; was made K.C.B. for his services in 1858, and received the thanks of Parliament, 1859; appointed major-general in 1861, having previously held local rank in China; colonel, royal engineers, 1862; colonel commandant and to the brevet of general, March, 1874; received the thanks of parliament, 1861, for the "skill, zeal, and intrepidity," shown in the operations which terminated in the capture of Pekin; was a member of the supreme council of India from 1861 till 1865, when he was appointed commander-in-chief of the Bombay army, with the local rank of general, 1865; made a lieut.-general in the army, and placed on the fixed establishment of general officers, May, 1867; appointed to conduct the expedition to Abyssinia, October following, on the successful conclusion of which, and capture of Magdala, he received the thanks of parliament; was created a peer; was granted a provision of 2,000L. per annum for himself and his next heir; he was also presented with the freedom of the city of London, and a sword of the value of 200 guineas; the freedom of the city of Edinburgh, &c.; appointed to the command in chief of the forces in India, with the local rank of general, Jan., 1870; and 5th ordinary member of the council of the governor-general of India, May following; appointed governor of Gibraltar, June, 1876; assumed the government, October, 1876.

NARRAWAY, J. J.—Employed in the Colonial office since January, 1831. For some years in the library, engaged in making analytical indexes to official correspondence; employed with the précis writer from Nov. 1859, until the abolition of that office in 1870; clerk in general department (copying branch), July, 1870.

NATAL, 1st BISHOP OF. (Founded 1853.)—RT. REV. JOHN WILLIAM COLENSO, D.D.—Educated at the Devonport proprietary school and at St. John's College, Cambridge, where he obtained a fellowship; graduated B.A. 1836 (when he was 2nd wrangler and Smith's prizeman); M.A., 1839; D.D., 1853; was fellow of St. John's College from 1837 to 1846; mathematical master at Harrow school from 1838 to 1842; rector of Forncett St. Mary, Norfolk, from 1846 to 1853; consecrated 1st Bishop of this see, 1853. Is author of 'Elements of Algebra,' and other works.
NAUDI, SALVATORE, LL.D.—Appointed a judge of the court of appeal, Malta, July, 1859. NAZ VIGILÉ, C.M.G. (1874).—Member of the council of government of Mauritius.

raw_text: NEEDHAM, SIR JOSEPH, KNT. (created 1873).—Called to the bar, Middle Temple, May, 1846; appointed chief justice of Vancouver Island, 1865; and chief justice of Trinidad, 1870.

events.position: Appointed assistant colonial surgeon to the governor of Ceylon

raw_text: NELL, G. M., M.D.—Appointed assistant colonial surgeon to the governor of Ceylon, 1871.

NELL, LOUIS.—Advocate of the supreme court of Ceylon; appointed acting deputy Queen's advocate for the southern circuit at Galle, in January, 1863; then appointed to the northern circuit in April, 1863; acting again at Galle from November, 1865; appointed deputy Queen's advocate for the eastern province, the 16th July, 1867; deputy Queen's advocate, north-western provinces, Jan. 1st, and deputy Queen's advocate, southern province, June, 1868; is author of a treatise on the 'Ceylon Courts of Requests,' used in the local civil service examinations, and of other publications connected with the legal profession; acting deputy queen's advocate for the midland circuit at Kandy, 16th November, 1873; acting deputy and queen's advocate for the midland circuit at Kandy, 16th Nov., 1873. Acting deputy queen's advocate for the island and law officer of the crown, 8th August, 1874; resumed duties at Kandy on the 19th October, 1874.

raw_text: NELSON, 2nd BISHOP OF.—RIGHT REV. ANDREW BURN SUTER, D.D.—Of Trinity College, Cambridge; was curate of St. Dunstan's-in-the-West, Fleet-street, London, 1856-7; was incumbent of All Saints, Mile End New Town; consecrated 1866.

raw_text: NELSON, NICHOLAS MERVYN.—Clerk on the establishment of the general post office at Sydney, New South Wales, Feb. 1, 1850; secretary to R. R. Smith and Edward James, Esqs., who arrived at Sydney as commissioners from the general post office, London, under instructions from the Home Government to inquire into and report upon the postal arrangements of the Australasian colonies, 1844; retired from the position of senior clerk on a pension, 1865.

NEUBRONNER, THOMAS.—In March, 1825, when the settlement of Malacca was transferred from the Dutch to the British Government, was appointed head clerk to the Resident, as well as coroner, Malacca. Was the interpreter of the Dutch, Portuguese and Malay languages in Her Majesty's court of judicature in 1840. Collector of assessment under the municipality in 1848. Deputy-sheriff in 1855. Acted as police magistrate and commissioner court of requests in 1858. In 1865; was appointed secretary of the municipality, and in conjunction with this, acted for nine months, in 1871, as police magistrate and commissioner of the court of requests.

NEVILL, HUGH.—Was at Magdalen College, Cambridge. Writer, Ceylon civil service, Sept. 1869; police magistrate, Point Pedro and Chavakachcheri, July, 1871; acting colonial magistrate, Galagedara, Dec. 1871; acting colonial magistrate, Gampola, June, 1872. NEWCASTLE (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP OF. Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. WILLIAM TYRRELL, D.D.—Educated at Charterhouse school, and at St. John's College, Cambridge, where he obtained a scholarship and was 4th senior optime; graduated B.A. 1831, and M.A. 1834; ordained 1832; appointed 1st Bishop of Newcastle in 1847 on the division of the bishopric of Australia.

(1875).—Appointed assistant colonial secretary of Mauritius, May, 1859; acting auditor-general, September, 1863; auditor-general, April, 1866; colonial secretary, 1868; has on several occasions administered the government of Mauritius. raw_text: NICHOLAS, REV. S.—Portuguese colonial chaplain, Colombo, Ceylon, 1864.

NICHOLSON, 1st BART. (Creat. 1859), KNT. BACHEL. 1852.—SIR CHAS. NICHOLSON, D.C.L.—Emigrated to Australia in 1834, and practised there as a physician; was elected a member of the first legislative council of New South Wales in 1843, and was three times chosen for the office of speaker (1845 to 1856); is provost of the university of Sydney.

NICOL, REV. GEORGE.—Ordained deacon at St. Paul's cathedral in 1849, by Dr. Bloomfield, late bishop of London; priest, the same year; appointed mathematical tutor at the Fourah Bay Institution, Sierra Leone, under the C.M.S., 1850 to 1856; appointed to the parish of St. Charles's, Regent, in the mountain district, 1856, and St. Patrick, Rissey, and Wellington, 1857; re-appointed to Regent, 1859; disconnected with nine other pastors from the C.M.S., and form the native pastorate under the superintendence of the bishop of Sierra Leone; appointed chaplain to the bishop in 1862; preferred to the chaplaincy of the Gambia, 1869; appointed colonial registrar, 1872; acting colonial chaplain of Sierra Leone, from July to Dec., 1874.

NIGHTINGALE, PERCY.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of the Victoria east division of the Cape colony, Aug. 1871; served as a volunteer in the Kafir war of 1850-1; clerk in H. M. dockyard, Simon's Town, 1853; appointed in March 1854, acting clerk in colonial secretary's department, and was employed in the governor's private office; September, 1854, clerk to resident magistrate, Port Elizabeth; June, 1857, clerk to civil commissioner, Bedford district; acted as civil commissioner and resident magistrate from January, 1860, to June, 1861; May, 1863, first clerk to civil commissioner, Swellendam district; July, 1864, civil commissioner and resident magistrate, Bedford district; June, 1867, civil commissioner and resident magistrate, Robertson district.
NOAD, W. R.—Was acting surveyor-general, Newfoundland, 1847; appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, Oct. 1852; acting surveyor-general from June, 1865, to Feb. 1866.

raw_text: NOONAN, JAMES LYONS.—Elected member of assembly for the district of Bonavista, Newfoundland, Nov. 1869; first appointed under government as commissioner of fisheries, 1870; colonial secretary and member of executive council, May, 1870.

NORMAN, GEORGE W.—Educated at the high school and college of Edinburgh; entered the colonial service in June, 1854, as landing waiter in the treasury department, Antigua; appointed harbour-master, June, 1856; coroner, August, 1865; re-appointed harbour-master, Sept. 1865; acted as provost-marshal from Oct. 1865, to May, 1866, and appointed a notary public in June, 1867; acting auditor and excise officer, Antigua, 1869.

NORMANBY, (GEORGE AUGUSTUS CONSTANTINE PHIPPS), MARQUIS OF,—succeeded to the title in 1863.—Privy counsellor (1851), K.C.M.G. (1874), appointed ensign and lieutenant, Scots fusileer guards, 1838; deputy lieutenant of Yorkshire, 1844; major, North York militia, 1841; resigned 1853; was treasurer of the Queen's household from Jan. 1853, to Feb. 1858, having been controller from July, 1851, to Feb. 1852; was added to the privy council on becoming controller; was M.P. for Scarborough from 1847 to 1851, when he was defeated after accepting office; regained his seat in July, 1852; appointed lieut.-governor of Nova Scotia, Jan. 1858; and resigned that appointment in Sept. 1863; appointed governor of Queensland, 1871 governor, New Zealand, Oct. 1874.

raw_text: NORRIS, WILLIAM.—appointed third clerk to police magistrate's office, Straits Settlements, Jan. 1858; promoted to the second clerkship, April of the same year, promoted to the chief clerkship to the court of requests on the 22nd Sept. 1860.

NORTHCOTE, 8th BART. (Eng.), created 1641. —Privy councillor, appointed 1866; C.B., civil 1851—Rt. HON. SIR STAFFORD HENRY NORTHCOTE. —Educated at Balliol College, Oxford, where he was first-class in classics and third-class mathematics, 1839; graduated M.A. 1842; created D.C.L. 1863; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1847; was one of the secretaries to the royal commission for the Industrial Exhibition of 1851; immediately after which he was made C.B.; was private secretary to Mr. Gladstone when President of the Board of Trade; financial secretary to the treasury from Jan. to June, 1859; president of the Board of Trade from July, 1866, to March, 1867; secretary of state for India, and president of the council for India from the last date to Dec. 1868; elected governor of the Hudson's Bay Company, Jan. 1869; captain 1st Devon Yeomanry Cavalry, 1852; a deputy-lieutenant of that county, 1854; was M.P. for Dudley from March, 1855, to April, 1857; for Stamford, from July, 1858, to May, 1866; when he was elected for North Devon; an elder brother of Trinity House. Chancellor of the exchequer in Mr. Disraeli's second administration, Feb., 1874.

events.text_span: Master, government schools, Mauritius, 4th July, 1862

raw_text: NORVILL, GEORGE.—Master, government schools, Mauritius, 4th July, 1862; superintendent of Orphan Asylum, Powder Mills, 1st July, 1869.

NOVA SCOTIA, 4th BISHOP of. Founded 1787. —RIGHT REV. HIBBERT BINNEY, D.D.—Educated at King's College, London; was afterwards successively scholar and fellow of Worcester College, Oxford, where he graduated 1st class mathematics, and 2nd class classics, 1842, M.A. 1844; appointed tutor of that college in 1846, and bursar in 1848; ordained a deacon, 1842, a priest, 1843, and consecrated 4th Bishop of Nova Scotia, 1851; is an honorary fellow of King's College, London; patron of the archdeaconries of Nova Scotia and New Brunswick, and visitor of King's College, Nova Scotia. This was the first colonial see founded by Great Britain; the diocese includes Nova Scotia, Cape Breton, and Prince Edward Island.

NOWELL, EDWIN CRADOCK (Tasmania).—Appointed clerk to the governor (Sir H. E. F. Young), 1st Oct. 1857; acting clerk of executive and legislative councils, 1st April, 1862; clerk of same, 13th May, 1864; government statistician, 1st Jan. 1867. Previously employed, from 1852, as temporary clerk in commissariat department Tasmania.

raw_text: O'BRIEN, G. T. M.—Educated at Westminster and Trin. Coll. Cambridge; writer Ceylon civil service; attached to colonial secretary's office, 1867; additional police magistrate, Kurunegalla, June, 1867; police magistrate, Harrispatta, Jan. 1869; acting second assistant colonial secretary, Feb. 1870; acting police magistrate, Colombo, Oct. 1870; assistant government agent, &c., Nuwara Eliya, April, 1871; acting assistant government agent, Hambantota, June, 1871; acting second assistant colonial secretary, Dec. 1871.

O'BRIEN, LIEUT.-COL. J. T. N.—Obtained his commission without purchase from Royal Military College, Sandhurst, May, 1847; gazetted ensign 67th regiment, 11th Sept. 1847; transferred to 70th foot, March, 1848; lieutenant 70th regiment, 5th May, 1850; captain 5th fusiliers, 2nd Feb. 1858; transferred to 20th regiment, 12th Feb. 1858; brevet-major, 26th April, 1859; major, unattached, 19th May, 1868; brevet lieut.-colonel, 3rd June, 1870; served during the troubles in the south of Ireland in 1848; embarked with his regiment for India in 1849, in which country he passed in the native languages, and also as a surveyor and a civil engineer, and where he held the following appointments:—Interpreter to a wing of the 70th regiment; adjutant and staff-officer of the Darjeeling Convalescent Depot and Station; interpreter to the 27th foot; assistant in the revenue survey, from which he was transferred to the public works, in which he rose, during his six years' service, to be executive engineer; served during the whole of the Indian mutiny; was, in 1857, at Peshawar, during the Ensofzaier expedition; disarmament of the native troops, destruction of the 55th, and outbreak and annihilation of the 51st native infantry, served throughout the Oude campaign of 1858-59, was deputy-assistant quartermaster-general to a column in the field; present, under the late Sir E. Wetherall, K.S.I. and C.B., at the storm and capture of Rampore Kussia; took part in the operations under Lord Clyde. leading to the surrender of Ameathie, evacuation of Shunkanpore and flight of Bein Malhoo; proceeded with the column under Brigadier Taylor, C.B., and Sir Hope Grant to Fyzabad; present at the crossing of the Gozra Afains of Muchleegawn and Kumdatoti, and pursuit of the rebels into Nepaul (mentioned and thanked in despatches, medal and brevet-major); in 1860 proceeded to Ceylon as assistant military secretary to the late Major-Gen. T. O'Brien, commanding the forces, and for two years governor of that colony, remaining there for three and a half years, when he returned to India, and was appointed to the staff of the Bengal army as brigade-major, Gwalior district, from which post he was invalided home in May, 1866, after a continuous service in the East of seventeen and a-half years; appointed in May, 1867, inspector-general of police, Mauritius; mentioned in despatches from the governor to the secretary of state, especially for his exertions during the dreadful hurricane of March, 1868, &c.; appointed poor-law commissioner and director of the Orphan Asylum, 7th February, 1870; in May, 1870, was selected by Sir Henry Barkly to be his extra A.D.C., and in this capacity was attached to H.R.H. the Duke of Edinburgh, during his stay in the Island.

O'BRIEN, JAMES W.—Appointed clerk and sorter in the imperial post-office, Trinidad, in 1842; held that appointment till 1851; in charge of entire department from 1851 to 1858; and was also employed during that period as clerk in the colonial post-office; acted as Her Majesty's packet agent in 1858; in Jan. 1860, on the amalgamation of the imperial and colonial post-offices, was appointed chief clerk of the general post-office; promoted to chief clerk in auditor-general's department 17th April, 1862; was acting postmaster-general from July to Sept. 1862; acting auditor-general from 4th October to 31st December, 1865; acting auditor-general, 1867; appointed to act as auditor-general in 1872; was secretary to the central road board, June, 1869, to March, 1875; acting warden of Couva ward union, and supervisor of the county of Carvin for the purpose of investigating the accounts of the late warden, from Oct. to June, 1873; again acted as auditor-general in 1874; acted as clerk of the legislative council, July to October, 1875.

raw_text: O'MALLEY, EDWARD LOUGHLIN.—Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, graduated; B.A., 1864; M.A., 1868; called to the bar (Middle Temple), 1866; joined the Norfolk circuit; unsuccessfully contested Bedford, 1868; attorney-general of Jamaica, 1876.

OMMANNEY, M. F.—Lieutenant R.E., educated at Cheltenham and R.M.A., Woolwich; entered the royal engineers, Jan., 1864; employed under the War Office and Admiralty, and at the royal military academy, from 1867 to 1874; private secretary to the Earl of Carnarvon, secretary of state for the Colonies, 4th March, 1874, to 1st January, 1877, when he was appointed a crown agent for the colonies.

raw_text: ONDAATJE, REV. S. D. J.—Appointed colonial chaplain of Jaffna, Ceylon, Dec. 1840; resigned that appointment, September, 1842; appointed colonial chaplain of St. Thomas' Church, Colombo, in that colony, Dec. 1861.

ONDAATJE, W. C.—Lic. Med. and Surg., Bengal College; appointed medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon; 3rd class, 1846; 2nd class, 1851; 1st class, 1856 and assistant colonial surgeon, 1858; assistant colonial surgeon, 1st class, 1867.

ONTARIO, 1st BISHOP of, 1861.—Dr. J. T. LEWIS, LL.D.—The diocese of Ontario comprises that part of West Canada which is bounded on the north by the Ottawa river, on the south by the Lake of Ontario; eastward from and including Prince Edward Island; on the south-east by the St. Lawrence river and the district of Vandreuil. It is a subdivision of the old diocese of Toronto. Its population is 370,000.

ORD, SIR HARRY ST. GEORGE, C.B. (1865), KNT., (1867).—Entered the royal engineers, 1837; promoted to be lieut., 1839; captain, 1846; brevet-major, 1854; lieut.-col., 1859; major-gen., 1869; served principally in the West Indies and coast of Africa; served as brigade-major in the combined French and English expedition to the Baltic in 1854; and was at the siege and capture of Bomarsund, for which he received brevet rank; was employed under the colonial office in 1855-6 as commissioner to the Gold Coast; and in 1856-57 and 1860 at the courts of Paris and the Hague, to assist the ambassador and minister in carrying out negotiations with respect to the French and Netherlands possessions on the coast of Africa; appointed lieut.-governor of Dominica, Aug. 1857; and governor and commander-in-chief of Bermuda, Feb. 1861; sent on a special mission to the settlements on the West Coast of Africa, Oct. 1864; governor of Straits Settlements. 1867 to Nov. 1873.

ORD, A. R.—Entered the 54th regiment, Dec., 1851; promoted lieutenant 86th regiment, 1855, and to a company, without purchase, in the 65th regiment, 1862; served during the Indian mutiny of 1857-8-9; was present at the action of Rota-ki-Lerai, at the battle and siege and capture of the town and fortress of Gwalior; commanded two companies 86th regiment at the siege and capture of the fort of Powrie; medal and clasp for Central India; served in the New Zealand war of 1863; medal for New Zealand, also medal for Bhootan; was present and commanded a detachment 65th regiment at the attack near the Williamson's clearing; commanded the Fort Alexander, and repulsed two attacks on that post; retired from the army in 1867; and appointed police magistrate, Malacca, Straits Settlements, 10th Jan., 1867; administered the lieut.-government Malacca, from 3rd July to 12th September, 1868; from 19th Dec. 1868 to 3rd February, 1869; from 25th May, 1872 to 22nd March, 1873; from 29th March to 19th April, 1874.

raw_text: ORPEN, A. R.—Civil commissioner, resident magistrate and sub-collector of customs, East London, appointed July 1, 1870; was resident magistrate and sub-collector of customs, Hondeklip Bay, 1862 to 1870; has served in the customs department since 1858.

ORPEN, FRANCIS HENRY SAMUEL, F.R.G.S.—Surveyor-general of the province of Griqualand West, 1872; one of the commission appointed June, 1850, for surveying the Orange River sovereignty; entered the service of the Griqua Chief, N. Waterboer, March, 1867, as civil commissioner, resident magistrate, and surveyor-general of the Settlement of Albania, and, on the cession of Griqualand West to the British Government, was appointed civil commissioner of the division of Hay (then Griquatown) Nov. 1871.

ORPEN, HENRY MARTYN HERBERT.—Served as captain commanding Colesbey native levies, from January, 1851, to August, 1853, during the Kaffir war of those years; also as district adjutant of North Victoria from April, 1852, to March, 1853; held several other military appointments during the same period; received a Kaffir war medal for services in the field; appointed to the customs' department at Port Elizabeth, Cape Colony, Dec. 1853; measuring surveyor under merchant shipping Act, Jan. 1856; landing surveyor, August, 1857; sub-collector and surveyor and comptroller of H.M. customs and navigation laws, Cape Town, October, 1867; appointed to temporary charge of customs' department, April, 1868, upon retirement of collector and principal comptroller until appointment of his successor.

OSBORN, M.—Clerk to resident magistrate at Ladismith, Natal; appointed Sept. 1865, resident magistrate of Newcastle in that colony.

O'SHANASSY, SIR JOHN K.C.M.G. (1874), (C.M.G. 1869).—Emigrated from Ireland to Australia soon after the first settlement of Port Phillip; he took a part in the municipal government of the city of Melbourne, and was elected a member of the first legislative council of the district; became chief secretary upon the 11th March, 1857, but retired from office at the end of the following month; held the same position from March, 1858, until Oct. 1859, and again from Nov. 1861, to June, 1863. Upon the eve of his departure for England, in 1866, a service of plate, worth more than £1,500, was presented to him by general subscription, in recognition of his public services.

DOUGLAS.—Acting clerk, immigration office, Trinidad, 14th May, 1872; temporary clerk, colonial secretary's office, 11th June, 1872; fourth clerk, colonial secretary's office, 1st January, 1873; acting inspector of police, 10th June, 1873; acting private secretary to Governor Longden, C.M.G., from 1st September, 1873, to February, 1874; appointed inspector of police, 24th December, 1873.
