raw_text: PACKER, CHARLES.—Graduated B.A. Trinity College, Cambridge, 1839; called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1841; appointed solicitor-general, Barbados, Jan. 1847; escheator-general, 1859; elected member of the general assembly for the parish of St. Andrew's, 1846; and has continued to represent the same constituency to the present time; elected speaker of house of assembly, 1861; member of legislative council, 1868; chief justice of Barbados, 1874.

PADMORE, W.—Clerk in customs, Jamaica, 1839; clerk and warehouse keeper, St. Christopher, 1842; acted as collector of customs, 1845 and 1848; clerk and assistant comptroller of customs and navigation laws, Barbados, 1853; acted as comptroller, 1854; pensioned, Jan. 1855, on abolition of customs in West Indies; notary public, St. Christopher, 1858; superintendent of immigrants, 1858; private secretary to Sir B. C. C. Pine; clerk to executive council and administrative committee, 17th June, 1865; clerk to office of auditor-general, 1866; provost-marshal, 1868. Resigned office of superintendent of immigrants, 1870.

PALMER, EDWARD.—Chief Justice of Prince Edward Island; has been attorney- and solicitor-general, and has sat in the local parliament.
PALMER, T. C., M.R.C.S.—Medical officer of district St. Kitts.

PARGITER, R. S.—Writer, Ceylon civil service, 1867; commissioner of requests, &c., Point Pedro, 1868; acting commissioner of requests, &c., Chavakachcheri, 1868; police magistrate, Kalpitiya, 1869, to continue to act at Chavakachcheri; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna, 1870; assistant government agent, Galle, Dec. 1871, continuing to act at Jaffna; acting assistant government agent Nuwera Eliya, May, 1872; acting landing surveyor, Colombo, June, 1872.

PARKER, C. S.—Educated at Eton and University College, Oxford; first-class in classics, M.A., fellow of University College; public examiner, 1859, 1860, and 1863; examiner in modern languages, 1862-3; curator of the university galleries; appointed private secretary to Mr. Cardwell, May, 1864, to July, 1866; M.P. for Perthshire, 1868; a special commissioner for public schools, 1868; and a commissioner for military education, 1869.

raw_text: PARKER, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1858.) SIR HENRY WATSON PARKER.—Was private secretary to Sir George Gipps when governor of New South Wales; was for many years a member and chairman of committee of the legislative council; was first minister and principal secretary and a member of the executive council of New South Wales. Unsuccessfully contested Greenwich, Dec. 1868.

PARKER, WILLIAM ALEXANDER.—Of the Scottish bar; is a member of the general council of the University of Edinburgh; in 1839 he was appointed assistant extractor of judicial records in her Majesty's general register house, Edinburgh; was honorary secretary of the architectural institute of Scotland; and corresponding associate for Scotland of the society of antiquaries of London, and of the genealogical and historical society of Great Britain; in 1853 was admitted a member of the faculty of advocates of Scotland; in 1859 was appointed captain of No. 1 battery, Midlothian coast artillery volunteers; in 1866 was appointed chief magistrate and member of the legislative council of her Majesty's forts and settlements on the Gold Coast, and judicial assessor to the native chiefs of the Gold Coast protectorate, Western Africa; in 1869 was appointed chief justice and judge of the Vice-admiralty court of St. Helena; member of executive council, 1871; chief justice, British Honduras, 1875.

Hambantota, 1856; acting principal collector of customs, 1866; acting government agent, southern province, 1868; government agent, north-western province, and acting principal collector of customs, 1869; assumed duties as government agent for the north-western provinces March, 1870; acting government agent, southern province, April, 1872; government agent, central province, July, 1872.
PATERSON, Dr. A. S.—Colonial surgeon, South Australia.

PATERSON, G. W.—Educated at the University of Glasgow, where he graduated M.A. 1857, being first in the order of merit in mathematics and natural philosophy; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Feb. 1859, having obtained the writership presented to the Glasgow University by the lord rector, Sir Edward Bulwer Lytton, then secretary of state for the colonies; acting police magistrate, Point Pedro, March, 1862, in which appointment he was confirmed, May, 1862; assistant government agent, Colombo, Jan. 1865; district judge, Tangalla, June, 1867; assistant government agent, Puttalam, June, 1873.

raw_text: PATEY, VICE-ADMIRAL CHARLES GEORGE EDWARD, C.M.G. (1874).—Entered the navy, 1824; lieut. 1836; served in the operations on the coast of Syria; commander, 1840; captain, 1846; appointed emigration officer at Glasgow, 1850; removed to Liverpool on the occasion of the great Australian emigration, 1851; received severe injuries whilst in command of H.M.S. 'Amphion,' 1853; appointed emigration officer at Londonderry, 1854; Glasgow, Oct. 1854; Plymouth, 1855 to 1857; was captain superintendent of the packet service, Southampton, from 1857 to 1864; rear-admiral, 1864; appointed administrator of the government of Lagos, 1866; administrator of the Gambia, Oct. 1866; governor, St. Helena, 1869 to 1873; vice-admiral, 1871; retired on pension, 1873.

PAUL, W. F. B.—Educated at Eton; accompanied the late Sir James Brooke, Rajah of Sarawak, to Borneo in 1860. Appointed civil commandant at Accra, West Coast of Africa, in 1873.

PAUNCEFOTE, SIR JULIAN, Knight Bach. (Created 1874).—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, May, 1852, and joined the Oxford circuit; appointed attorney-general of Hong Kong, May, 1865; was ex officio member of the executive and legislative councils of the colony; appointed acting chief justice of the supreme court in 1869 and again in 1872; prepared "The Hong Kong Code of Civil Procedure," and other important ordinances now in force in the colony relating to law reform and the constitution of the courts, and to emigration, extradition, and other subjects; received the thanks of the legislative council of Hong Kong and the honour of knighthood for his public services to the colony; appointed chief justice of the Leeward Islands in 1873, and an assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies, 28 September, 1874, to 29th June, 1876; an assistant under-secretary of state, foreign office, 30th June, 1876.

PEEL, Privy Councillor (1857), K.C.M.G. (1869).—RIGHT HONOURABLE SIR FREDERICK PEEL, 2nd son of the late Sir Robert Peel, 2nd Bart.—Educated at Harrow and at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he was 1st class in classics, 1845; was called to the bar at the Inner Temple, 1849; was under-secretary for the colonies from Nov. 1851 to March, 1852, and from Dec. 1852 to Feb. 1855; under-secretary for war from 1855 to 1857; is a deputy-lieut. of Warwickshire; was M.P. for Leominster from Feb. 1849 to July, 1852, and for Bury, Lancashire, from July, 1852 to April, 1857; re-elected for Bury, May, 1859, and appointed secretary to the treasury, 1860, railway commissioner, 1873.

raw_text: PENNELL, E. B.—Appointed, after a competitive examination, a clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, April, 1859; promoted to the 3rd class, April, 1863; private secretary to Mr. Forster, under-secretary of state for the colonies, until July 6th, 1866; also to Sir C. Adderley, from that date to October 1, and to Lord Blachford from 1 Oct. 1866; promoted to be an assistant clerk, 20th May, 1867; 1st class clerk, 30th Sept. 1872; proceeded to Paris on special service, 1874.

raw_text: PHAYRE, MAJOR-GENERAL SIR ARTHUR PURVES, K.C.S.I. (1867), C.B. (1863).—Served in the 7th Bengal native infantry, and became lieut.-col. Bengal staff corps; made major-general in the army in 1871; was British commissioner for Arracan, and afterwards for many years chief commissioner at British Burmah; was created a knight of the Star of India for his long and efficient services in Burmah, where he concluded several important treaties; retired, and returned to England, 1867; appointed governor of Mauritius, Oct. 1874.
PHILIP, M. M.—Solicitor-general, Trinidad, March, 1871; acted as attorney-general, June, 1873, to July, 1874.

PHILLIPPO, GEORGE.—Admitted to the honourable society of the Inner Temple, April, 1859; obtained a certificate of honour of the first class, Jan., 1862; called to the bar, Hilary term, 1862; admitted to the Jamaica bar, June, 1862; practised at the Jamaica bar until Feb., 1868; during that time was professionally engaged before the royal and special commissioners appointed in consequence of the serious disturbances in that island, and of the measures of repression taken by the Government; was detained in England for some months by the Jamaica committee in order to give evidence as to the laws of Jamaica in the various prosecutions instituted by that body; appointed queen's advocate, Sierra Leone, March, 1868, and acting judge of the court of summary jurisdiction during the absence on leave of Mr. Justice Huggins, August, 1868; appointed attorney-general of British Columbia, March, 1870; appointed on a commission to collect, revise, and publish a complete edition of the laws of that colony, 1871; prepared the proclamation necessary for bringing the new constitution of that colony into operation under the order in council, 1870, whereby representative institutions were to some extent conceded; nominated a non-elective member of the legislative council under the new constitution, Dec., 1870; prepared the address from the legislature of British Columbia to Her Majesty for union with Canada, and prepared and passed through the legislative council, under the direction of the government, "The Constitution Act, 1871," and the several other measures necessary to enable British Columbia to enter into confederation with Canada, with full representative institutions and under "responsible government;" puisne judge of the supreme court of British Guiana, May, 1871; went to Gibraltar on special service, Sept., 1873; appointed acting attorney-general and judge of the court of requests, Gibraltar, during the absence on leave of F. Solly Flood, Esq.; remained in Gibraltar after the return of Mr. Flood, on special service from Dec., 1873, to Feb., 1874; appointed junior puisne judge, Straits Settlements, Dec., 1873; and senior puisne judge, Straits Settlements, March, 1874; attorney-general, Hong Kong, August, 1876.

H.—Acting chief clerk, registrar-general's department, Trinidad, Oct., 1870, and April, 1871, and May, 1872; acting superintendent registrar, births and deaths, Oct., 1870, and April, 1871; and May, 1872; was for many years chief clerk in the marshal's department; chief clerk in registrar-general's department, and superintendent registrar of births and deaths, July, 1873.

PHILPOTT, EDWARD.—Civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Uitenhage division, Cape of Good Hope; appointed 1867. Was clerk to the magistrate at Port Elizabeth, 1837 to 1842; resigned on account of ill-health; re-appointed clerk to civil commissioner of Port Elizabeth, March, 1848; appointed civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Alexandria, August, 1857; ditto Cradock, 1866 to 1867.

PICKWOAD, ROBERT WILLIAMS.—Appointed private secretary to W. W. Cairns, Esq., when lieut.-governor of British Honduras, 28th July, 1870; continued in that capacity from June, 1871, to June, 1872, to Colonel Harley, who administered the government during Mr. Cairns' leave of absence; appointed clerk to the colonial secretary and clerk to the legislative council of British Honduras, 28th March, 1872; continued as private secretary to Mr. Cairns on His Excellency's return from leave in June, 1872, to March, 1874; private secretary to Captain Mitchell, R.M., during his administration of the government, to Major Mundy on his arrival in May, 1874, to September of the same year, and again subsequently to Captain Mitchell. Appointed keeper of cemeteries, 16th November, 1874.

PIERRE, LOUIS PHILIP.—Appointed clerk of the peace of the county of Caroni, Trinidad, November, 1860; clerk of the peace of the county of Victoria, with the town of San Fernando, in 1869; clerk of the peace of Port of Spain and the western district of the county of St. George in June, 1869; appointed acting stipendiary magistrate of the county of St. David, and ward of Blanchisseuse in the county of St. George, and warden of the Toco Ward Union, in June, 1872; and on the 14th of January, 1874, stipendiary magistrate of the county of St. David and ward of Blanchisseuse, warden of the Toco Ward Union, and acting stipendiary magistrate of Arouca and Arima in the eastern district of the county of St. George.

PINE, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1856), K.C.M.G. (1871).—SIR BENJAMIN CHILLEY CAMPBELL PINE. —Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A. 1834, M.A. 1840; called to the bar, Gray's Inn, 1841; appointed Queen's advocate at Sierra Leone, 1841; acting governor there, 1848, accompanied Capt. Monypenny, R.N., to the Sherbro river, and destroyed a strong stockaded fort, from which outrages had been committed on British trading vessels. In 1849, again proceeded to the Sherbro, in conjunction with Capt. Dunlop, R.N., and a detachment of troops, and put an end to the civil war, which had ravaged the country for years; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1849; 1851 received the thanks of her majesty's government for the measures he took to preserve peace in the colony during the Kaffir war; in 1852 despatched a force, consisting of Cape mounted rifles, two companies of the 45th regiment, and 900 Zulus, to the aid of British troops in the Orange River sovereignty, who were reported to be acting on the defensive against an overpowering force of hostile tribes; superintended in person the raising of the Zulu force, and accompanied the expedition over the Drackenbent mountains in mid winter; received the thanks of Sir Henry Smith for these services; in 1855, led a force of mounted volunteers against the Amabaca tribe, numbering 2,000 men, and completely enforced their submission; governor and commander-in-chief of the Gold Coast settlements, 1856; lieut.-governor of St. Christopher's, May, 1859. Is author of some articles in the Encyclopedia Britannica on the 'African Colonies;' acting governor, Antigua, 1866; governor in-chief of the Leeward Islands, Feb. 1869. Created K.C.M.G. for his services in connection with the confederation of the Leeward Islands; lieut.-governor of Natal, 1873, until 1875; when he retired on a governor's pension.

POLE, REGINALD C.—Was educated at Marlborough college; employed from July, 1859, till Sept. 1861, in the surveyor-general's department, during the latter part on the trigonometrical survey, Ceylon; acting landing and tide surveyor, at Galle, April, 1862; acting assistant collector of customs at Jaffna; appointed landing surveyor, Galle, 1st April, 1863; acting assistant government agent, Jaffna; June, 1863; confirmed, 1865; passed final examination in languages (Tamil), laws and accounts, Jan. 1868; police magistrate and commissioner of requests, Galle, Feb. 1868; assistant government agent, Matalé, Nov. 1868; police magistrate of Kandy, Sept. 1869; acting assistant government agent of the north-western province at Puttalam, 1st Dec. 1871.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH.—Appointed auditor-general, British Guiana, 1860; was financial accountant from 1853 to 1860, when the office was abolished.
POLLARD, WILLIAM BRANCH, jun.—Colonial civil engineer, British Guiana.

raw_text: POLLARD, W. F. B.—Appointed surgeon to penal settlement, British Guiana, 1873.

POPE, RICHARD.—Born 1827; called to the bar of Lower Canada. 1854; assistant editor of the "Lower Canada Law Reports," 1855 to 1861; president of the Chaudière gold mining association, 1866 to 1871; clerk in corresponding branch, department public works, and private secretary to minister, 1872 until 21st October, 1873, when appointed clerk of the crown in chancery for Canada; is a major in the militia; organized Quebec volunteer rifle association at the time of the "Trent" affair; won first prize medal of Literary and Historical Society, Quebec, for best "Essay on Canada," 1853; author of "Canadian Minerals and Mining Interests," 1857; "Gold Fields of Canada," in 1858; "Notes on Immigration" and "Mining and Agricultural Labour in Canada," 1859.

PORTER, NEALE.—Presiding magistrate of Anguilla from March 1869 to May 1871; acting colonial secretary from May 1871, to Oct. 1871; acting lieut.-governor of Dominica from Oct. 3, 1871, to March 31, 1872; acting president of Dominica from April 1 to June 13, 1872; president, Montserrat, June 13, 1872.

POTTS, MOSES A.—Educated at the Sierra Leone Grammar School. Entered the service in H.M. commissariat at Sierra Leone in June, 1853, and served uninterruptedly in that department till January, 1860; served also in the barrack department at that station; was acting second clerk in the colonial treasury of that settlement from July 1865, to January, 1866; appointed extra clerk in the same department in January, 1866; acting house and land-tax clerk from April 1866, to June 1868; appointed house and land-tax clerk, Aug. 1869; promoted to be second clerk in the colonial treasury, after a competitive examination, on the 15th Sept. 1870; census master for the year 1871; was acting first clerk and cashier from January to May, 1873, and again from August of the same year.

QUEBEC, 4th Bishop of.—Dr. J. W. WILLIAMS, 1863.—Educated at Crewkerne school, and Pembroke College, Oxford; graduate B.A., 1851, 3rd class classics; curate of High Wycombe, Bucks, 1851; proceeded to Canada to open grammar school in connection with Bishop College, Lennoxville; is a professor in that college; consecrated 4th Bishop of Quebec, 1863; the diocese was first constituted by patent, 28th June, 1793; and comprised Upper and Lower Canada and their dependencies. It now includes districts of Quebec, Three Rivers, St. Francis and Gaspé, and contains 43 parishes.

RAE, JOHN, M.A.—Educated at University of Aberdeen, where he passed as master of arts, 1832, law student, from 1831 to 1839; arrived in New South Wales, 1842, was appointed town clerk of Sydney, 1843; a commissioner for the City of Sydney, 1854; secretary to the railway department, New South Wales, 1857; under secretary for public works and commissioner for railways, 1861.

raw_text: RAMPINI, CHARLES.—Civil law prizeman, University of Edinburgh, 1865; member of the faculty of advocates, Scotland, June, 1865; stipendiary magistrate for the parishes of Portland, St. Thomas-ye-East and St. George's, Jamaica, March, 1867; judge of the Port Antonio district court, Nov. 1867; judge of the Mandeville district court, May, 1868; is coroner for the parishes of Manchester and Clarendon; and a justice of the peace for those of St. Thomas and Portland.

RANDELL, HENRY LLOYD.—Surgeon major, served with the 62nd regiment in the Crimea from the 4th July, 1855, including the siege and fall of Sebastopol, and assault of the Redan on the 8th September (medal with clasp, and Turkish medal); subsequently served in the West Indies, and went out to Canada with the expeditionary force in January, 1862; and afterwards served in India, till March, 1867; in Nov., 1867, was placed on the seconded list, on obtaining the appointment of principal civil medical officer, Straits Settlements.

RAWSON, SIR RAWSON WILLIAM, K.C.M.G., (1875) C.B. (1858).—Appointed a clerk in the board of trade, Jan. 1829; served as private secretary to the vice-president, Mr. C. Poulett Thomson, 1830-34; to the president, Mr. Alex. Baring, 1834; and to the vice-president, Mr. W. E. Gladstone, 1841-42; acted as secretary to the pilotage commission, 1834-36; appointed civil secretary to the governor-general of Canada, Aug. 1842; transferred, upon the abolition of that office, to the treasurership of Mauritius, Jan. 1844; appointed colonial secretary of the Cape of Good Hope, May, 1854; appointed governor of the Bahamas, Nov. 1864; received, Nov., 1865, a dormant commission as lieutenant-governor of Jamaica, to act in the absence or incapacity of the captain-general and governor-in-chief; governor-in-chief of Windward Islands, from 1868 till 1875. Retired on pension.

READ, THE VEN. ARCH. J. H., D.D.—1st ARCHDEACON OF PRINCE EDWARD ISLAND, 1860.—Ecclesiastical commissary, 1858; formerly of St. John's College, Cambridge, and B.D., 1852; D.D., 1857, of King's College, Frederickton, New Brunswick.

RICHARDS, ALBERT MORTON, Q.C.—Born, 1822; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1848; to that of British Columbia, 1871; created a Q.C., 1863; sat in legislative assembly, Canada, from 1863 to 1864, and in house of commons from 1872 until January, 1874; was a member of the executive council, Canada, and solicitor-general for Upper Canada, 1863-4; appointed attorney-general for Rupert's Land, and the north-west territories, October, 1869; legal adviser in British Columbia for the dominion government, 1874; lieutenant-governor of the province of British Columbia, 20th July, 1876.

RICHARDS, STEPHEN.—Born, 1820; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1844; created a Q.C., 1858; elected a bencher of law society of Ontario, 1871; sat in Ontario assembly from 1867 to 1875, and was commissioner of crown lands from July, 1867, to July, 1875; and provincial secretary and registrar from latter date to Dec., 1871.

RICHARDS, HON. WILLIAM BUELL.—Born 2nd May, 1815; educated at the Johnstown district grammar school; called to the bar of Upper Canada in Michaelmas Term, 1837; elected a bencher of the Law Society, 1849; created a Q.C., 1850; was a member of executive council, Canada, and attorney-general for Upper Canada, from 28th October, 1851, to 21st June, 1853, when appointed a puisne judge of the court of common pleas, where he remained until 22nd July, 1873, when promoted to be chief justice of that court; appointed chief justice of Ontario, 16th November, 1868; chief justice of the supreme court of the dominion, 8th October, 1875; and deputy-governor of the dominion, 29th July, 1876; was arbitrator on behalf of Ontario, for the settlement of the north-western boundary of the province, 1874.
RICHARDSON, SIR JOHN LARKINS CHEESE, KNIGHT BACH. (1875).—Speaker of the legislative council of New Zealand; lieut.-colonel of the local militia, and late brevet major of Her Majesty's Indian forces.

ROBINSON, BRYAN.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin; barrister at colonial bar of Nova Scotia and Newfoundland, 1829, at the latter of which he practised for upwards of twenty-nine years; appointed in 1858, judge of the supreme court of Newfoundland.

ROBINSON, SIR HERCULES, G.R.—G.C.M.G. (1875), (KNT. BACHEL., 1859), (K.C.M.G. 1869).—Served for some time in the 87th Fusiliers; on his retirement from the army was actively engaged during the Irish famine, 1846-9, under the commissioners of public works and poor law board in Ireland; was appointed in 1852 chief commissioner to inquire into the fairs and markets of Ireland; was appointed president of Montserrat, 1854; lieutenant-governor of St. Christopher, 1854; with which he held the dormant commission of governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands; appointed governor, of Hong Kong, June, 1859. Was a member of the commission appointed in the autumn of 1863, to inquire into the financial condition of the Straits Settlements; governor of Ceylon, 16th May, 1865; governor of New South Wales, 1872. Employed on special service in 1874-5, in connection with the cession of the Fiji Islands to Great Britain.

ROBINSON, WILLIAM, F.R.G.S.—Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, 1st Jan. 1854; assistant junior, Nov. 3, 1854; promoted to 3rd class, June, 1858; was private secretary to Mr. Herman Merivale, from June, 1858, to May, 1860; to Lord Blachford, from May, 1860 to March, 1862; when he was promoted to be an assistant senior. Private secretary to the Right Hon. Edward Cardwell, from April 4 to June 17, 1864; appointed, Nov. 1869, to represent the Colonial Office on the East African Slave Trade Commission; senior clerk, 1st July, 1870; and a principal clerk, 30th September, 1872; appointed one of the colonial committee for the Vienna universal exhibition of 1873, and special commissioner for the Crown colonies, and superintendent of the colonial court at that exhibition; is editor of the report upon the British colonies represented there; was one of the committee for securing a permanent colonial museum in London; appointed lieutenant-governor of the Bahama Islands, Oct. 1874, and governor, 1875.

ROBINSON, WILLIAM C. F., C.M.G. (1873), —Was private secretary to Sir Hercules Robinson in the government of St. Kitts and Hong Kong, from 1855 to 1860; president of Montserrat, 1862; administered the government of Dominica from the 5th Jan. to the 18th Oct. 1865; governor and commander-in-chief of the Falkland Islands, 23rd May, 1866; governor of Prince Edward Island, 5th July, 1870, to Nov. 1873; nominated governor-in-chief of the Leeward Islands, 1874; appointed governor of Western Australia, 7th October, 1874.

ROGER, CAPTAIN ARCHIBALD.—Ensign 2nd royal Lanark militia, Aug. 1859; gazetted to the 2nd West India regiment, Nov. 1860; lieutenant, Aug. 1861, captain, Oct. 1866; exchanged to the 1st battalion 17th regiment, Feb. 1867; sold out of the service, Jan. 1870; was private secretary to Mr. Bayley, governor of the Bahamas, Dec. 1860, to July, 1863, and fort adjutant, Jamaica, Dec. 1863, to Sept. 1864; appointed acting police magistrate of the rural districts, St. Christopher, Dec. 1872; confirmed in that appointment, June, 1873; appointed district magistrate, May, 1874.

ROSE, SIR JOHN, BART. (1872) K.C.M.G. (1870).—Was called to the bar of Lower Canada in 1842, and obtained a patent as Queen's counsel in 1847. In the year 1857 he accepted the position of solicitor-general of Canada, and was elected to parliament for the city of Montreal. Among the measures introduced by him as solicitor-general was that for the abolition of the Usury Laws. He became receiver-general of Canada in August, 1858, and afterwards minister of public works in 1859. In that capacity he had the control of the arrangements during the visit of His Royal Highness the Prince of Wales to Canada, and in the following year, until his resignation, those for His Royal Highness Prince Alfred. In 1867, at the first election of the Dominion parliament, he selected the county of Huntingdon, for which he was elected. The finance minister having resigned at the opening of the first session, that office, with a seat in the privy council, was accepted by him in November, 1867. He was requested by the governor-general, on behalf of Her Majesty's government, to make a confidential examination into the alleged grievances of the province of Nova Scotia to the financial terms conceded to that province in entering the union, and for a redress of which they had appealed to the Imperial Parliament. He gave it as his opinion that there were grounds for the complaints of Nova Scotia, and he recommended the extending of large financial concessions to them. He was selected in 1869 to proceed to Washington to confer with the government of the United States on the subject of reciprocal trade, the question of the fisheries, copyright, patent laws, &c., &c.; the navigation of the St. Lawrence, and extradition of criminals. In 1864 he was named by Her Majesty commissioner on the part of Great Britain, under the convention with the United States of America, of July, 1863, for the settlement of claims arising out of the Oregon Treaty of 1846, the duties of which were brought to a close in September, 1869. He resigned the office of minister of finance of Canada in October, 1869.

Eng.—Chief medical officer and director, public hospital, Jamaica, 14th May, 1870; served formerly on the army medical staff; appointed parochial medical attendant, Spanish Town, 1866; medical superintendent, Cooly depôt, 1867; a government medical officer, December, 1868; acted from December, 1868, to August, 1869, as resident medical officer and registrar, public hospital, in the room of two medical officers; medical superintendent, lepers' home, from February to May, 1870.

ROUND, FRANCIS R., B.A.—Educated at Marlborough and Balliol College, Oxford, where he obtained a double second class in moderations, and a second in classics in the final examination, 1868; appointed, after a competitive examination, clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, February, 1869; 2nd class clerk, 30th September, 1872; accompanied Sir P. Julyan, K.C.M.G., C.B., to Mauritius in 1873 as secretary on the occasion of that gentleman's special commission of inquiry into the civil establishment and general expenditure of that colony.

ROWE, SAMUEL, C.M.G. (1874), Surgeon-Major.—Engaged on the West Coast of Africa since 1862 and as medical officer and chief of the staff with Sir J. H. Glover during the Ashantee war, 1873-4, in which capacity he accompanied Sir J. H. Glover's force into Ashanti and through Coomassie; despatched on special service to the Gold Coast, November, 1874; lieutenant-governor, West Africa Settlements, 1875; administrator of the Gambia, 1875; conducted two expeditions into the Sherbro country in 1876; governor of the West Africa Settlements, 1876.

raw_text: ROZARIO, RAFAEL A.—Appointed interpreter to the supreme court, Hong Kong, 1857.

RUPERT'S LAND, 2nd BISHOP OF. Appointed 1865.—RIGHT REV. ROBERT MACHRAY, D.D.—Late dean and fellow of Sidney Sussex College, Cambridge, and vicar of Madingley; graduated in 1855, when he was 34th wrangler. The diocese of Rupert's Land is 37,000 square miles in extent; and has a population of 200,000 souls, and comprehends the whole of the Hudson's Bay Company's settlements (or Prince Rupert's Land); it was erected into a see in 1849, and endowed by a legacy from the late W. Leith, Esq. RUSHWORTH. EDW. EVERARD, C.M.G. (1872).—D.C.L. and late fellow of St. John's College, Oxford; appointed a clerk in the inland revenue office, Somerset House, 1842; was private secretary to Sir Gaspard Le Marchant, in the governments of Newfoundland and Nova Scotia, from 1847 to 1855; president administering the government of Montserrat from 1855 to 1861; auditor-general of Mauritius, 1861. Was acting director of the railway department; a member ex officio of the council, and commissioner of admiralty court in that island; appointed March, 1866, government secretary, British Guiana; administrator, government of Trinidad, April, 1866; financial secretary, Jamaica, Oct. 1866; administered the government of Jamaica during Sir J. P. Grant's six months' absence in 1870; administered the government of British Guiana during the absence of Governor Scott in 1873.
RUSSELL, THE VERY REV. A.—Dean of Adelaide.

RUSSELL, EARL (creat. first Earl, Aug., 1861) G.C.M.G. (1869).—JOHN RUSSELL.—Was M.P. for Tavistock from 1813 till Mar. 1817, and also from July, 1818, till March, 1819; for Huntingdonshire from 1820 till 1826; and for Bandon Bridge from January, 1827, till 1830; was made a privy councillor, November 22, 1830; paymaster of the forces from December 16, 1830, till December 23, 1834; M.P. for Tavistock from December, 1830, till Dec. 1832, when he was returned for South Devon, which county he represented till May, 1835; appointed one of the ecclesiastical commissioners for England, Aug. 15, 1836; secretary of state for the home department from April 18, 1835, till Aug. 30, 1839; M.P. for Stroud from May 21, 1835, till June, 1841; secretary of state for the colonies from Aug. 30, 1839, to Sept. 3, 1841; M.P. for the city of London, from July, 1841, to 1861; first lord of the treasury from July 6, 1846, till Feb. 26, 1852; secretary of state for foreign affairs from Dec. 28, 1852, till Feb. 20, 1853, when he retained a seat in the cabinet, but held no appointment; lord president of the council from June 12, 1854, till Feb. 7, 1855; appointed to be the unpaid charity Commissioner for England and Wales, July 8, 1854; sent on a special mission to Vienna, Feb. 11, 1855; appointed one of a committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, Feb. 28, 1855; secretary of state for the colonies from March to July, 1855; appointed secretary of state for foreign affairs, June 19, 1859; and one of the committee of council to superintend the application of any sums of money voted by parliament for the purpose of promoting public education, July 6, 1859; was in attendance upon Her Majesty when she proceeded to Coburg in Sept. and Oct. 1860; prime minister, Nov. 1865, to July, 1866.

raw_text: RUSSELL, JAMES.—Graduated B.A. at Queen's University, Ireland, 1863; M.A., 1873; LL.B., obtaining university gold medal, 1874; called to the bar at Lincoln's-inn, Easter term, 1874; appointed to Hong Kong cadetship, August, 1865; government interpreter, 1st December, 1867; private secretary, February, 1868; police magistrate, 1870.
RUSSELL, THE VEN. ARCHDEACON, P. T.—Vicar-general of the Roman Catholic Church, South Australia.

events.text_span: Collector of customs, Natal, 1853.

raw_text: RUTHERFORD, G.—Collector of customs, Natal, 1853.

RUTHERFORD, W.—Employed on the unfixed establishment of the surveyor-general's department, Ceylon, from 15th March, 1859; assistant surveyor, January, 1871. ST. HELENA, 2nd BISHOP OF (founded 1859)—RIGHT REV. THOMAS EARLE WELBY, D.D.—Appointed rector of Newton, Lincolnshire, in 1847, but resigned that living on becoming a colonial chaplain at the Cape; was archdeacon of George Town, from 1856 till May, 1862, when he was consecrated bishop of St. Helena. This colony was part of the see of Cape Town previously to the 6th June, 1859, when it was constituted a diocese, together, with the islands of Ascension and Tristan d'Acunha; the population is about 7,000. ST. HILL, RICHARD AUGUSTUS.—Native of island of St. Vincent; assistant judge of supreme court of judicature in that island; appointed, 1866 first appointment in colonial service as coroner, 1842; resigned that office in 1851; has been a justice of the peace for the island 34 years; one of a bench of magistrates selected to carry out the duties of police magistrate for the Kingston district, during the abeyance of the Act in 1850; acted as magistrate (police) for the Grenadines district in 1859; for several years an assessor and appraiser of taxable objects, &c. ST. PERN, J. G.—Clerk to the stipendiary magistrate, Savanne, Mauritius, Sept. 1853; appointed district clerk, 1860.

SALMON, C. S.—Appointed sub-collector of customs and J. P., British Sherbro, 23rd April, 1866; acted as manager and coroner of British Sherbro from 23rd April, 1866, to 8th Feb. 1867, and from 18th Dec. 1867, to 6th March, 1868; land commissioner for Sherbro, 4th April, 1867; acted as collector of customs, Sierra Leone, from May to Oct., 1868; also as registrar for shipping; collector of customs, Gold Coast, 28th Dec., 1869; acted as chief magistrate and judicial assessor, Gold Coast, from 8th April to 2nd Nov. 1870; chief civil commissioner, Seychelles (Mauritius), 1874.

SAMUEL, SAUL, C.M.G. (1874).—Late postmaster-general of New South Wales; vice-president of the executive council and representative of the government in the legislative council of that colony; has also held office in four previous administrations as minister for finance and trade; was first elected in October, 1854, to represent the united counties of Roxburgh and Wellington in the old legislative council, before the introduction of responsible government in the colony; he afterwards represented various constituencies in the legislative assembly, his last election being for the metropolitan electorate of East Sydney, and continued in that branch of the legislature until May, 1872, when he resigned to accept a seat in the legislative council to represent the government in that house. Mr. Samuel, during his parliamentary career has been the author of several measures which have become law — amongst others, the Navigation Act. In 1873, Mr. Samuel was appointed special commissioner by the government of the colony to visit New Zealand, England, and America, to make arrangements for the establishment of a mail service between the United Kingdom and the Australasian colonies by way of the United States; he resigned, 1875, with the rest of the Parkes ministry.

SANDERS, EDWARD HOWARD. — Formerly a 1st lieutenant in the Imperial Austrian 6th Dragoons, in which regiment he served for six years, leaving them in 1855 for the purpose of accepting a commission in Her Majesty's service, with the view of serving his own country in the field during the Crimean war; he was appointed lieutenant and adjutant of the 2nd Hussars, British German Legion, commanded by Lord Elphinstone, and served in them until they were disbanded; appointed stipendiary magistrate and gold commissioner of British Columbia, in April, 1859; also member of the legislative council.

events.text_span: Appointed clerk in treasury, South Australia, 26th June, 1854
secretary to Lands Titles Commissioners, 1st Dec., 1862

raw_text: SANDERSON, FREDERICK JAMES. — Appointed clerk in treasury, South Australia, 26th June, 1854; secretary to Lands Titles Commissioners, 1st Dec., 1862; and secretary to attorney-general, 1st Dec., 1870.

SANDFORD, SIR FRANCIS RICHARD, KNT. BACHEL. (creat. 1863) C.B. — Educated at Glasgow University and Balliol College, Oxford; was 1st class in classics, 1846, and graduated M.A. Oxon and LL.D. Glasgow; entered civil service as examiner privy council office, 1848, assistant secretary, 1854; knighted by patent for his services as secretary to the commissioners for the exhibition of 1862; assistant under-secretary of state for the colonies, December, 1868, to 30th January, 1870; secretary to committee of council on education, 1870.

events.text_span: appointed 1874.

raw_text: SANDFORD, THE RIGHT REV. CHARLES WALDEGRAVE, D.D. — A bishop exercising episcopal functions over the same region as the former bishop of Gibraltar; appointed 1874.

SANGUINETTI, FREDERICK S. — Is a second-class clerk in the office of the colonial secretary, Jamaica. Was appointed in Sept. 1863, a clerk in the office of the secretary to the main road commissioners; clerk in the office of the executive committee Feb. 1864; on change of constitution, appointed to present office, 16 Sept. 1866. Has acted on three occasions as a 1st class clerk.

raw_text: SARGEAUNT, W. C., C.M.G. (1875). — Appointed clerk in the office of the secretary of state for the colonies, Feb. 1848; was permitted whilst on the establishment of that office to hold the appointment of colonial secretary of Natal from 1853 to 1858; was a member of the executive and legislative councils of that colony, a justice of the peace for the same, and lieut.-colonel of the Natal carbiniers, a corps of cavalry yeomanry; returned to the colonial office in 1858, and was appointed acting lieut.-governor of St. Vincent in 1859; returned to the colonial office, and was promoted an assistant clerk, 1860; appointed crown agent for the colonies, 1862.

events.text_span: called to the bar, Lincoln's-inn, 1848

raw_text: SARGENT, SIR CHARLES, KNT. BACH. (1861). — Educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, 1843; 5th wrangler; called to the bar, Lincoln's-inn, 1848; formerly chief justice of the Ionian Islands; now one of the puisne judges, Bombay.

SARTORIUS, MAJOR REGINALD WILLIAM, C.M.G., V.C. (1874). — 6th regiment of Bengal Cavalry, appointed to the Order of St. Michael and St. George for service in the Ashantee war, 1873-4, under Sir J. H. Glover, and received the Victoria Cross for an act of conspicuous gallantry in saving a wounded sergeant-major of Houssas.

raw_text: SAULEZ, S. V. — Late lieutenant H.M.'s 103rd R. B. Fusiliers; appointed assistant-inspector Gold Coast constabulary, November, 1874; acting district commissioner Dix Cove, July, 1875.

SAUNDERS, F. R. — Appointed assistant surveyor, Ceylon, 1857; acting assistant agent, Trincomalee, July, 1860, in which appointment he was confirmed, 1862; promoted to be assistant to government agent at Kandy, August, 1865; district judge, &c., Ratnapura, Nov. 1865; assistant to the government agent, Western Province, at Ratnapura, October 1867; acting inspector-general of police, March, 1872; acting inspector-general of prisons, Dec. 1872; confirmed, 1874.

SCHMIDT, LOUIS EDWARD. — Appointed in Aug. 1861, clerk to stipendiary magistrate, Moka, Mauritius; Sept. 13, 1861, clerk in the colonial secretary's office; July, 1863, corresponding clerk; registrar, April, 1866.

SCHOLTZ, ROBERT IRWIN. — Was appointed clerk to the civil commissioner and resident magistrate of King William's Town, Cape Colony, 10th April, 1868, also distributor of stamps; appointed clerk in the deeds office at Klip Drift, Griqualand West, 5th August, 1872; chief clerk to the resident magistrate of Du Toits Pau, Griqualand West, 15th September, 1872; chief clerk to the attorney-general of Griqualand West, and clerk of the peace for the province, 10th July, 1873. SCHÜRER, GEO. H. — Appointed clerk in the Treasury of British Honduras, July, 1869; keeper of the public cemeteries, September, 1871; private secretary to lieutenant-governor Cairns, July, 1872; performed duty as clerk to the colonial secretary of that colony for a short time, in conjunction with duties in the colonial treasury; justice of the peace, September, 1874; assistant collector of customs of the Gold Coast, November, 1874, to May, 1875; sent on special mission to Quittah, May, 1875, and received thanks of Governor-in-Chief for services in connection with the district accounts; assistant-collector and acting treasurer of Lagos, June, 1875, to January, 1876, also ex officio registrar of shipping and shipping master; commissary of taxation, British Guiana, Feb., 1876.
SCOTT, CHAS. W. — Employed as an extra clerk in the colonial office from 1840 appointed clerk in the registry department in 1852; clerk in the general department, July, 1870.

SCOTT, SIR JOHN, K.C.M.G. (1874). — Was employed in the foreign office on the affairs of the North American boundary commission, from 1840 to 1843; was appointed, in 1843, secretary to the boundary commission, established in conformity with the Ashburton Treaty of 1842, to define and mark out on the spot the boundary agreed upon in that treaty, in which operations he voluntarily undertook an active share in the explorations, surveys, and other field duties of the commission, and for which he received the special approbation of the secretary of state; at the termination of this commission, in 1848, was appointed surveyor-general to the new settlement of Labuan; became acting lieutenant-governor and treasurer in 1849, and was appointed lieut.-governor of the colony by commission dated Nov. 1850; in March, 1856, was transferred from the government of Labuan to that of Natal, which government he held to the end of Dec. 1865; appointed governor of British Guiana,

SCOTT, RICHARD WILLIAM, Q.C.—Born, 1825; called to the bar of Upper Canada, 1848; created a Q.C., 1867; sat in the legislative assembly of Canada from 1867 to 1873, and while there carried the separate school act of Upper Canada; returned to the Ontario Assembly at Confederation, 1867; retired on his entrance into the Dominion Government, November, 1873; called to the senate, 13th March, 1874; was speaker of the Ontario assembly from 7th to 21st December, 1871; a member of executive council and commissioner of crown lands for same province, from latter date until 7th November, 1873, when sworn of the Queen's privy council; appointed secretary of state of Canada, 9 January, 1874; is ex officio registrar-general of Canada, and has civil control of the mounted police force, enrolled for service in the north-west territories of the Dominion.

raw_text: SCOTT, W. H.—Local superintendent, Mauritius railways, 20th Jan., 1864; general manager of railways, May, 1870.

SEALY, SIR JOHN, K.C.M.G. (1874), (C.M.G. 1869).—Educated at Exeter College, Oxford, where he was 2nd class in classics, 1829; graduated M.A., 1833; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, in Nov. 1833; was solicitor-general of Barbados from 1841 to 1846, when he was appointed attorney-general of that colony. Is also chancellor of the diocese; was for many years a member of the house of assembly; resigned his seat, and was appointed a member of council in 1858. Retired on a pension, 1874. Resigned seat in council, 1876.

raw_text: SEALY, THOMAS.—Entered the army, May, 1841; accompanied the 10th hussars to India, 1846; from thence, via Egypt, to the Crimea; present at the fall of Sebastopol (medal and clasp and Turkish medal); entered civil service convict department, April, 1857; appointed superintendent of prisons, Trinidad, 23rd May, 1870; selected to fill the post of superintendent of the penal settlement, Massaruni, British Guiana, 26th October, 1873.

SEMPER, JOHN RAWLINS.—Queen's counsel, Montserrat, appointed 1844. Is a member of the executive council, solicitor-general, Antigua; chief justice of St. Kitts, Nevis, and Anguilla, 1867; and judge of the court of appeal; 1st puisne judge, Leeward Islands, 1873.
SEMPER, R.E., M.R.C.S.—Medical officer, district 6; acting president of legislative assembly St. Kitts, and member of executive council.

events.text_span: appointed chief-justice of Mauritius in May, 1860

raw_text: SHAND, SIR CHAS. FARQUHAR, KNT. (creat. 1869).—Advocate, LL.D., Edinburgh, took academic honours every year, and repeatedly the first; author of "The Practice of the Court of Session," &c.; was an advocate depute, and counsel for the lords of the treasury, and the hon. commissioners of woods and forests in Scotland; chairman of the united industrial or ragged schools; director of the philosophical institution; and captain in the volunteer rifles, Edinburgh; appointed chief-justice of Mauritius in May, 1860; is also judge of the vice-admiralty Court.
SHARPE, HENRY.—Provost-marshal, Grenada; appointed 1855; with which office was combined, November, 1874, that of inspector of police.

raw_text: SHARPE, W. E. T.—Educated at Trinity College, Dublin, where he obtained a scholarship, and graduated B.A. 1857; appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, 1857; assistant government agent, Kurunégalla, 1860; and assistant agent, Kandy, 1861; promoted to district judgeship of Batticaloa, Aug. 1865; assistant Government agent, Badulla, 1868; acting government agent, north-western province, June, 1873.

SHAW, EDWARD WINGFIELD.—Retired captain royal navy, when lieutenant of H.M.S. "Plumper"; on the west coast of Africa, in 1853 and 1854, during an attack of Ashantees served on shore with Gold Coast corps as gunnery officer, and instructed that regiment in field and battery drill (their own adjutant and instructor having been killed by an explosion); for this he received the thanks of the secretary of state for the colonies; appointed private secretary to governor-in-chief of Leeward Islands in 1863; administered the government of Montserrat from April, 1865, to March, 1866; lieutenant-governor, Malacca, December, 1868.

raw_text: SHAW, GILBERT.—Admitted to practice as an attorney-at-law in the supreme court of Jamaica in February, 1829; appointed clerk to the magistrates of Clarendon, 1st January, 1839; custos rotulorum of Vere, March, 1847; clerk of the peace, and magistrates' clerk of Vere, November, 1848; elected member of the house of assembly for Clarendon in December, 1859; appointed clerk of the vestry for Vere, March, 1862; served as a commissioner to inquire into and report upon the working of the immigration laws in May, 1867; appointed clerk of the Spanish Town district court, November, 1867, and provost-marshal general of the island, 1st June, 1871 (now abolished); appointed a stipendiary magistrate for St. Catherine's, July, 1874; acted as judge of the Spanish Town district court.

SHAW, HENRY.—Appointed treasurer of St. Vincent, Feb. 1864; acting clerk of courts and keeper of records, 1850 (Honduras) Queen's advocate, full commission, May 30, 1851; coroner, Feb. 1857; nominee of the crown in the legislative assembly, 1861; was acting treasurer of St. Vincent from July, 1863, until his confirmation in that appointment in 1864.

raw_text: SHAW, HENRY BRIDGWATER.—Inspector of prisons, Jamaica, appointed April 1, 1863; has acted as agent-general of immigration.

SHAW, JOHN.—Late lieut. Longford militia; appointed chief clerk, customs department, 1863; acting collector, 1864, and collector and controller of customs, Sierra Leone, 1865, which office he at present holds; appointed also a commissioner for settling claims to lands, 1864; while on leave in England, during the Ashanti war, placed his services at the disposal of the secretary of state, and was by him directed to proceed to Lagos and assume the duties of collector of customs and colonial secretary in room of Captain Lees, transferred to the Gold Coast; administered the Government of Lagos from July to September, 1874; appointed treasurer of Sierra Leone, 1875.

raw_text: SHEPPARD, WILLIAM TYNDALL.—Educated at Harrow and Trinity College, Cambridge (graduate, 1855); appointed clerk, railway department, South Australia, in April, 1856; clerk of court of insolvency, August, 1858; secretary to attorney-general, February, 1866; secretary to commissioner of public works, July, 1869; secretary to commissioner of crown lands and immigration, August, 1874; under treasurer, May, 1875.

raw_text: SHEPSTONE, JOHN.—Assistant magistrate, P. Maritzburg, Natal, 1865.

SHEPSTONE, SIR THEOPHILUS, K.C.M.G. (1876), (C.M.G., 1869).—Appointed, Jan. 8, 1835, head-quarter interpreter of the Kafir language at the Cape, and served on the staff of Sir Benjamin D'Urban, governor and commander-in-chief, during the Kafir war of 1835; appointed clerk to the agent-general for native tribes on the frontiers of the Cape Colony, 1836; sent to accompany the first military expedition from the Cape to Natal, 1838; created justice of the peace under the Act, 6 & 7 Will. IV., to 25th degree south latitude; returned to the Cape Colony in 1839; appointed resident diplomatic agent to the Hlambe tribes, and superintendent of Fingoes at Fort Peddie, on the frontiers of Cape Colony, 1839; appointed diplomatic agent to and over the tribes inhabiting the colony of Natal, 1845; captain-in-chief of the native force in Natal, 1848; appointed to control, revise, and direct the administration of justice among the natives in that colony, according to native law, 1849; proposed and personally carried out a scheme for the taxation of the native population in the colony, which yielded about 10,000l. the first year, and now amounts to upwards of 16,000l. per annum; appointed judicial assessor Natal, under ordnance No. 1, 1855; was captain of the native police corps, from its appointment in 1848 to 1854, when it was disbanded; and has been engaged on various special missions and services in the colony not here enumerated; appointed secretary for native affairs, 1856; and member of the executive and legislative councils in that colony in the same year; proceeded on a special mission in 1873 to crown the king of Zululand; repaired to England August, 1874, to confer with the secretary of state on the native policy of the Natal government; returned in December, 1874; revisited England, July, 1876, as delegate from Natal to the London South African conference; appointed special commissioner in connection with the territories adjoining the colony, 5th Oct., 1876.

SHERIFF, ROBERT FFRENCH.—Called to the bar, Inner Temple, in November, 1862; admitted to practice at the Antigua bar in 1863; appointed crown member of the legislative council there, 1867, and one of her Majesty's counsel, 1869; administered the government of Montserrat from May, 1869, to July, 1870; elected a representative of the presidency of Antigua in general council, Leeward Islands, May, 1872; acting chief justice, St. Christopher and Nevis, October, 1873; attorney-general, Leeward Islands, 1874.

raw_text: SKEEN, G. J. A.—Appointed extra assistant government printer, Ceylon, 1869; acting government printer, 22nd June, 1875.

SKINNER, ALLAN MACLEAN.—Called to the bar, June, 1867; appointed, after competitive examination, to the civil service of the Straits Settlements, Nov. 1868; appointed acting sheriff of Singapore, after qualifying in Malay, Jan., 1871; confirmed in that appointment, April, 1871; appointed acting magistrate of province Wellesley, Penang, April, 1871; accompanied, July, 1871, the expedition to Salangore; appointed, in January, 1873, inspector of schools, to report on the state of education; he is also inspector of prisons, hospitals, and asylums for the colony; attended the governor in the Island of Pangkor, during the negotiations with the headmen of Perak, in January, 1874; in August gazetted acting assistant colonial secretary and clerk of the councils; appointed in Feb., 1876, acting postmaster-general.

SKINNER, T., C.M.G. (Creat. 1869).—Was formerly a major in the army, joined his regiment in Ceylon in 1819; was selected by Sir Edward Barnes to open a section of the road between Colombo and Kandy, 1820; employed from this time till 1837 on the staff in laying out lines of communication throughout the colony, and in carrying out a trigonometrical survey and sketch of the mountain zone, comprising the Upper Kandian provinces, and subsequently the greater portions of the island. The result of these surveys is published in two maps, by the late General John Fraser, deputy quarter-master-general of Ceylon; appointed to the charge of the combined departments of surveyor-general and civil engineer, Ceylon, 1838; commissioner of roads, 1841; to which, in 1850, the civil engineer department was added; acting auditor-general, 1859, and again 1865. Retired July, 1867.

SKINNER, T. E. B.—Appointed a writer to the government of Ceylon, Oct. 1860; acting assistant agent, Kurunégalla, March, 1862, in which appointment he was confirmed in the following May; assistant agent at Nuwara Eliya, 1863; acting district judge, Batticaloa, January, 1866; acting central province, 1867; postmaster-general, 1872.

SMITH, ALBERT JAMES, Q.C.—Born, 1822; called to the bar of New Brunswick, 1847; is a Q.C.; sat in New Brunswick assembly from 1852 until 1867, when returned to house of commons, where he remains; was a member of the executive council, New Brunswick, from 1856 to 1863, and also for a short period in 1866; attorney-general from 1862 to 1863, when he retired from the Government; held same office in his own administration, 1865; was a delegate to England with the present Judge Fisher, 1858, on the subject of the intercolonial railway; to the same country, with the present Chief Justice Allan, on public business, 1865; and to Washington, with Hon. Messrs. Galt (now Sir A. T.), Howland and Henry, on the subject of reciprocal trade, Jan., 1866; declined the chief justiceship of New Brunswick, 1866; the lieut.-governorship of the same province, 1873; and the ministership of justice of the Dominion, June, 1874; sworn of the privy council, and appointed minister of marine and fisheries, 7th Nov., 1873.

SMITH, CECIL CLEMENTI.—Educated at St. Paul's School and at Corpus Christi College, Cambridge; graduated as B.A. 1862, M.A. 1868; elected student interpreter, Hong Kong, March, 1862; appointed registrar general 13th March, 1865; acting police magistrate, June, 1865; acting colonial secretary, 1867; appointed member of executive council, 1869; acting colonial secretary, February, 1872, to December, 1873; appointed treasurer, 1871; acted as colonial secretary in Oct. 1874, and also from 11th March, 1875.

SMYTH, MAJOR-GEN. EDWARD SELBY.—Served as brigade-major to the forces in the Southern Concan and Sawant Wanee country during the campaign of 1844-45, and was present at the attack and capture of several strong stockades, as well as in the operations before the mountain forts of Monohur, and at their final assault; also at the forcing of the Kirwattee Pass, and subsequent occupation of the country below the Ghats: served also in the Kaffir war of 1851-52 (medal), and mentioned in general orders for coolness and intreppidity in command of a column in action in the Fish River Bush—brevet major; with the expedition north of the Orange River in 1852-53, afterwards as deputy-assistant quarter-master-general of the 2nd division; and subsequently as D.-Adj.-Gen. and D.-Q.-M.-Gen. to the forces in South Africa from January, 1854, to July, 1860; commanding the troops, Mauritius, from 13th April, 1870; assumed the administration of the government, 3rd June, 1870; and from 19 August till 29 September, 1871, was acting secretary to the government in the eastern provinces, Cape of Good Hope, in 1859, whilst employed there as Quarter-master-general of the army, and was inpector-general of Irish militia from 1861 till October, 1867; appointed a special magistrate for the county and city of Dublin in 1867; appointed to command the Canadian militia, 1874.

SNOWDEN, FRANCIS.—Educated at Rugby and University College, Oxford; graduated M.A. in 1854; in the same year was called to the English bar; practised on the western circuit and at the Bristol, Bath, and Salisbury borough sessions; in 1862 was appointed counsel for the treasury at the Salisbury sessions; in 1868 made a supernumerary revising barrister; acted as deputy county court judge; appointed senior magistrate, Straits Settlements, Nov. 1871; senior puisne judge, 1873; puisne judge, supreme court, Hong Kong, March, 1874.

raw_text: SOMERSET, FITZROY MOLYNEUX HENRY, son of the late Right Honourable Lord William G. H. Somerset.—Is an officer of royal engineers, and a colonel in the army of 25th January, 1871; was appointed a member of the legislative and privy councils of Bermuda, 1874; and was acting governor and commander-in-chief of that colony from May to November, 1874; has also served in Canada and the Mediterranean.
flags: name_block_extra:son of the late Right Honourable Lord Wi

raw_text: SOUTHEY, R., C.M.G. (1872).—Colonial secretary, Cape of Good Hope, 22nd July, 1864; was captain of corps of guides during the Kaffir war of 1834-1846; resident agent and magistrate to the Kaffir tribes of Umkai, Novebi, and Seyolo, at fort Murray, province of Queen Adelaide, 1836; secretary to high commissioner, Dec. 1847 to 1850. During this period was present at the battle of Boomplaats, and subsequently acted as president of war tribute commission in the Orange River Sovereignty; civil commissioner and resident magistrate of Swellendam, Feb. 1850, to May, 1852; acting secretary to government, Cape Town, May, 1852, to May, 1854; civil commissioner and resident magistrate at Swellendam, May, 1854, to Feb. 1855; secretary to lieut.-governor of Graham's Town, March, 1855, to Jan. 1859; auditor-general, Jan. 1859, to April, 1859; secretary to lieut.-governor, Graham's Town, April, 1859, to Aug. 1860; acting colonial secretary, Cape Town, Aug. 1860, to Jan. 1862; treasurer-general, Jan. 1862, to July, 1864; lieut.-govr. of Griqualand, Dec., 1872, till 1875.

SPENCER, H. H.—Appointed 1st officer to the colonial steamer "Dover," May, 1860; commander, 1863; served in the Baddiboo, Quiah, and Tubabcolong wars; transferred to Sierra Leone, Nov., 1866, as harbour-master and superintendent of lighthouse.

raw_text: SPERLING, R. A.—Appointed assistant civil engineer and commissioner of roads, Ceylon, 1857; acting provincial assistant, north-western province, 1866.

SPEEDY, T. C. S.—Served in Her Majesty's 81st regiment from 1854 to 1860. During the Indian mutiny of 1857-1858 was present at the taking of Mungultana, near Sitana, during the Eusufzaie expedition under Sir Sidney Cotton, K.C.B. (medal); served as adjutant of the 10th regiment Punjaub infantry at Dera Ismail Khan, north-west frontier of India; was British vice-consul at Massowah, Abyssinia, in 1864; served in the New Zealand militia from 1864 to 1867; was captain and adjutant of the Waikate militia; served in the Abyssinian expeditionary force, 1867-68, on Sir Robert Napier's staff—intelligence department; present at the action of Arogie and capture of Magdala (medal); appointed by Her Majesty guardian to Prince Alamaya Simyen, son of the late King Theodore, of Abyssinia; district superintendent of Oudh police till 15th March, 1871; appointed commissioner of police, Penang, 14th April, 1871; now assistant British resident at Laroot.

SPROAT, GILBERT MALCOLM.—Educated for the India civil service; went to Vancouver Island in 1860; founded the settlement of Alberni; was magistrate and collector of customs there; was joint secretary of the committee in London for improving the condition of merchant seamen; chairman of the association for watching the affairs of British Columbia; was agent-general for British Columbia, 1872 to 1876; appointed, Sept., 1876, joint commissioner by the governments of Canada and British Columbia for the settlement of the Indian land controversy; is author of Scenes and Studies of Savage Life and other works; won the first prize of 200l. for the best essay on the British opium policy in India and China, Sir Bartle Frere, Sir C. Trevelyan, and Sir L. Mallet, being the judges.
SPROTT, ROBERT PORTER, M.D., Edinburgh.—Medical officer, No. 1 district, St. Vincent.

STEPHEN, KNT. BACHEL. (Creat. 1846), C.B. (civil), 1862; K.C.M.G., 1874. — SIR ALFRED STEPHEN. — Was called to the bar at Lincoln's Inn, 1823; appointed solicitor-general of Van Diemen's Land in 1825; attorney-general of that colony in 1832; one of the judges of New South Wales in 1839; and chief justice there in 1854, on which occasion he received the honour of knighthood. Retired; appointed lieutenant-governor New South Wales, November, 1875.

raw_text: STEPHENSON, F. L. — Appointed junior clerk in the treasury, Jamaica, 1st June, 1852; promoted in the same department, 1861; appointed landing-waiter of H. M. customs, Falmouth, March, 1865, and locker and gauger at the same port, 1866; transferred as clerk and warehouse keeper to Montego Bay, Nov., 1867; appointed acting sub-collector of customs at that port, Aug., 1868; transferred to collector-general's office, Kingston, as customs statistical clerk, July, 1869; appointed acting chief clerk in the same office, 1870; and appointed to present office of chief clerk in H. M. customs at the port of Kingston, December, 1870.

raw_text: STONE, R. SIDNEY, M.D., L.R.C.P., Lond., M.R.C.S. — Formerly house surgeon at St. Bartholomew's hospital, London; assistant surgeon and resident surgeon, civil hospital, Mauritius, Dec. 1864; government medical officer, vaccinator, and commissioner in lunacy for the district of Flacq, 1870; was chairman of the local board of health of Flacq until its abolition in 1874.

STRACHAN, W. H. P. F. M., LIEUT.-COLONEL. — Entered the army in 1852; lieutenant, 1853; captain, 1862; major, 1871; lieut.-colonel, 1874; on the staff as fort adjutant, at Bahamas, 1859 to 1861; inspector-general of police and a justice of the peace for the settlement of Bathurst, province of Gambia, 1867, where he also acted for some time as queen's advocate; commanded troops at Bahamas; British Honduras, member of executive and legislative councils, British Guiana and Gold Coast, member of both councils, and acting police magistrate of the latter colony in 1875.
STRACHEY, WILLIAM. — Appointed by Earl Grey in 1848 to be précis writer to the colonial office; retired 1870; was formerly in the Bengal civil service.

STRAHAN, CAPT. GEO. C., R.A., C.M.G. (1875). — Entered the royal artillery, Oct. 1857; was appointed, Jan. 1859, aide-de-camp to the Right Hon. W. E. Gladstone, M.P., when lord high commissioner of the Ionian Islands; and Feb. 17th was appointed in the same capacity by the late Sir Henry Storks, G.C.M.G., K.C.B.; acted as chief secretary to the government of Malta during a portion of the year, 1868-9; colonial secretary, Bahamas, 1868; acting governor, Bahamas, from April, 1871, to May, 1873; appointed administrator of the government, Lagos, 1873; governor of the Gold Coast Colony, July, 1874; governor of the Windward Islands, 1876.

STRANGWAYS, HENRY BULL TEMPLER. — Visited South Australia in 1853-4; called to the bar at the Middle Temple, June, 1856; a member of the South Australian Parliament, Dec., 1857, to Feb., 1871; attorney-general, May, 1860, to May, 1861; commissioner of crown lands and immigration, May, 1861, to October, 1863, and again, March, 1865, to October, 1865; attorney-general and premier, October, 1868, to May, 1870; carried through the Parliament the first Act for the sale of land on credit, and arranged for the construction of the Central Australian telegraph line; appointed a magistrate for Somersetshire, 1871; permitted by the Queen to retain the title of Honourable in South Australia as a late member of the executive council.

STRAUBENZEE, G.C.B. (1875), (K.C.B. 1858). — GENERAL SIR CHARLES THOMAS VAN STRAUBENZEE. — Born at Malta, 17th Feb., 1812; entered the army, as ensign, 28th Aug., 1828; lieutenant, 22nd Feb., 1833; captain, 10th March, 1837; major, 27th Aug., 1843; brevet lieut.-colonel, 30th April, 1844; lieut.-colonel, 11th Nov., 1851; colonel, 20th June, 1854; major-general, 14th Aug., 1860; lieut.-general, 27th March, 1868; general, 29th April, 1875. Was present with his regiment, the 39th Foot, throughout the campaign in Coorg, 1834, and during the occupation and annexation of that territory. As major of the 39th, he succeeded to the command of the regiment during the action of Maharajpore, Gwalior, 29th Dec., 1843, for which battle he received his brevet lieut.-colonelcy, and remained in command during the disarmament of the Gwalior forces. As brevet lieut.-colonel and major of the Buffs, he was appointed to the command of a field force in Ireland, encamped at Pilltown and Carrick-on-Suir, for the purpose of operating between Waterford and Clonmel, and consisting of a battery of artillery, two troops 4th light dragoons, seven companies of the Buffs, and two of the 85th light infantry, during the Smith O'Brien troubles in 1846. As lieut.-colonel, he commanded the British contingent in Greece during part of 1854 and 1855, and in April, 1855, proceeded to the Crimea, where he was appointed to command a brigade, and remained till July, 1856; was present at the two assaults on the Redan, in June and September; at the former he commanded a separate brigade, at the latter the 1st brigade light division, and was slightly wounded. On his return from the Crimea, he was appointed to the command of the 1st brigade, Dublin division, and remained there till ordered, in 1857, to China, to command a brigade, but before the commencement of operations he succeeded to the command of the forces; he commanded at the capture of Canton by assault, on 29th Dec., 1857, and was in command at all subsequent operations till relieved, in March, 1860. In 1861, Major-General Van Straubenzee proceeded to Bombay, and commanded the northern division, and, on Lieut.-General Sir Wm. Mansfield's appointment to command the Indian army, Sir Charles succeeded to that of Bombay, which he held nearly a year, till the arrival of Sir Robert Napier, in 1865; and returned to England early in 1866. Was appointed governor and commander-in-chief of Malta, in 1872. For the battle of Maharajpore, he received a brevet lieut.-colonelcy and the bronze star of Gwalior; for the Crimea, medal with clasp, companionship of the Bath, officer of the Legion of Honour, Sardinian and Turkish medals, and 3rd class of the Medjidie; for China, K.C.B., medal and clasp. He was appointed colonel of the 47th Foot in 1865, and was removed to the colonelcy of the 39th Foot in 1867, and in 1875 was appointed a grand cross of the Bath.

events.text_span: called to the bar, Lincoln's-inn, 6th June, 1857
appointed Queen's advocate, West Africa Settlements, Oct., 1874.

raw_text: STREETEN, WILLIAM WARREN.—Educated at Tunbridge School; called to the bar, Lincoln's-inn, 6th June, 1857; appointed Queen's advocate, West Africa Settlements, Oct., 1874.
STUART, JOHN.—Clerk of the house of assembly, Newfoundland, 1855. Also secretary to the board of works, 1858.

STUART, MELVIN VICTOR DEBMONT.—Appointed temporary clerk in treasury department, Nassau, Bahamas, May, 1864; junior clerk, July, 1865; acting collector of revenue, Harbour Island, 1866, and Eleuthera, 1867; acting collector at Nassau, from July, 1870, to Jan., 1874; also from Dec., 1874, to Feb., 1875; 2nd clerk, or book-keeper, March, 1875; measuring surveyor of shipping from Nov., 1864, to Oct., 1875; examining officer at Gold Coast, 1875.

SULLIVAN, FRED.—Appointed to governor's secretary's office, Jamaica, Christmas, 1852; employed therein as clerk from Feb., 1853; in 1859, being then chief clerk, acted as private secretary to Governor Darling, from May to Nov.; in May, 1860, confirmed in that appointment; held that office until March, 1861, when he was transferred to present office of chief clerk in the post office department; has acted on several occasions as postmaster for Jamaica; appointed postmaster for Jamaica, 1870.

SUTTON, CHARLES.—Entered the army, 1846; rose to be a lieutenant without purchase in Dec., 1853; acting barrack-master from 1st Sept., 1860, to 23rd June, 1862, when he retired; present at the assaults on Sebastopol, on the 18th June and 8th Sept., 1855. Has a Crimean medal and clasp, and Turkish medal; inspector of prisons, Mauritius, 1862; inspector of police, Bahamas, June, 1864.

raw_text: SWETTENHAM, F. A.—Appointed to the civil service, Straits Settlements, 1870; is attached to lieut.-governor's office, Penang; assistant collector of land revenue, 1 Sept., 1872.

SWETTENHAM, J. A.—Educated at Clare Coll., Cambridge, where he obtained a scholarship in May, 1867; writer, Ceylon civil service, 1868; acting police magistrate, Harrispattu, Feb. 1870; police magistrate, Kayts, Sept. 1870, continuing to act at Harrispattu; acting assistant government agent, Galle, Dec. 1871; acting landing and tide surveyor, Galle, June, 1872; assistant collector of customs, Jaffna, Oct. 1872, to continue to act at Galle; acting district judge, Matara, April, 1873. SYDNEY (AUSTRALIA), 2nd BISHOP OF.—Founded 1847.—RIGHT REV. FREDERIC BARKER, D.D.—Educated at Grantham School and at Jesus College, Cambridge, where he graduated A.B. 1829, M.A. 1835, D.D. 1854; became incumbent of Upton, Cheshire, 1830; of St. Marv's, Edge Hill, Liverpool, 1835; and of Baslow, Derbyshire, 1854; consecrated 1854; is author of "A Commentary on Twenty-six Psalms," &c.; is metropolitan bishop in Australia. The diocese originally included the whole of Australia, and bore that name; but in 1847 it was restricted to the central portion of the colony, and called by its present title, while additional bishops were nominated for Newcastle, Adelaide, Melbourne, and Perth; by patent, 19th October, 1854. The bishop is a metropolitan bishop, whose suffragans are the bishops of the above dioceses.

C.—Chairman of the board of inspectors of asylums and prisons, Canada; secretary of board of registration and statistics in 1861; deputy to the minister of agriculture, 11th Aug., 1864; appointed to same office under dominion government, July, 1867.

TASMANIA, 2nd BISHOP of. Founded 1842.— RIGHT REV. CHARLES HENRY BROMBY, D.D.— Educated at Uppingham and St. John's College, Cambridge, where he graduated B.A., 1837, his name appearing in both triposes; M.A. 1840, D.D. 1864; consecrated 1864; is author of numerous educational works, including 'The Church Student's Manual,' 'History and Grammar of the English Language,' 13 volumes of 'Papers for the Schoolmaster,' &c.; became incumbent of St. Paul's, Cheltenham, 1843; and principal of the Cheltenham Normal College, 1847, in the erection of which he took a prominent part.

TAYLOR, EDWARD BARNETT ANDERSON.—Is a member of the Bahamas bar; entered civil service in 1847; was private secretary and colonial aide-de-camp to Mr. Mathew, when governor of the Bahamas, also to Mr. Gregory, Sir Alexander Bannerman, and Mr. Bayley; secretary to the board of education from 1851 to 1861; captain-commandant of the New Providence volunteer rifle corps, 1853; clerk, police court, 1851; provisionally appointed police magistrate, 1861, and confirmed in 1862; acting receiver-general and treasurer from 14th Oct., 1862, to 24th July, 1863; acting colonial secretary from 5th April to 19th July, 1867; acting assistant justice in Sept., 1869; acting chief justice from Oct., 1869, to Jan., 1870, and again from 21st July, 1873, to 19th Jan., 1874; acting colonial secretary from 22nd June, to 1st Dec., 1874, and confirmed in that appointment on the 5th Dec., 1874; is ex officio senior member of the executive council; is one of the trustees of the public bank, chairman of board of public works, and one of the appointed trustees of the Nassau public library, reading-rooms and museum.

Retired 29th Sept. 1872.

TEMPLER, P. A.—Appointed writer to the government of Ceylon, Sept. 1863; deputy fiscal, Kandy, 1864; police magistrate at Panadura, Oct. 1865; acting assistant government agent, Trincomalee, Nov. 1865; acting assistant government agent, Kandy, January, 1866; acting police magistrate, Gampola, April, 1866; assistant collector and landing surveyor, Jaffna, April, 1866; acting assistant government agent, Colombo, July, 1866; acting district judge, Ratnapura, Sept. 1866, confirmed Oct., 1867; acting assistant government agent and district judge, &c., Mannar, May, 1870; fiscal, central province, Sept. 1872.

THIBOU, JOSEPH THEODORE.—Entered the colonial secretary's office, Antigua, on March 5, 1863; appointed second clerk, July 29, 1864; second clerk registrar's office, April 12, 1867; acted as clerk in marshal's office from June, 1870, to February, 1871.

events.text_span: Appointed marshal, Trinidad, 1870.

raw_text: THOMAS, MARTIN.—Appointed marshal, Trinidad, 1870.
THOMAS, M.—Chief clerk to commissioner of police, Penang, March 1, 1859, until March 1, 1869; was then appointed to be chief clerk in import and export office.

THOMSETT, HENRY G.—Entered the royal navy, 1840, and served actively for twenty-one years; was engaged in a combined attack of British and French naval and land force under Commodore Fanshawe on a horde of pirates at Basis, Jeba River, West Africa, 12th December, 1849; during two periods of service on the African station, assisted in the capture of twenty-seven slave vessels in the bight of Benin and coast of Loando; commanded H.M.'s ship 'Princess Charlotte' at Hong Kong, from February, 1858, to Sept. 1861; acting harbour master, 17th March to 26th Nov. 1860, and again 1st March to the 31st Aug. 1861, when he was appointed harbour master, marine magistrate, and emigration and customs officer, at Hong Kong, July, 1861, and was placed on the retired list of the royal navy in 1870.

THOMPSON, ALEX. J.—Entered the colonial secretary's offices, Bahamas, as a junior clerk, Jan. 1847; secretary to board of health during the prevalence of cholera, 1852; promoted to be deputy colonial secretary, 1854; gazetted as a captain in the militia in 1853; appointed a master in chancery in 1859; a justice of the peace and coroner in 1860; acted as colonial secretary and registrar of records in 1860 and 1862; and in the latter year was returned as a member of the legislature; provost-marshal, October, 1862; is ex-officio marshal of the courts of chancery, divorce, bankruptcy, ordinary and admiralty sessions; is also a member of the board of health, and a commissioner of the Asylum.

THOMPSON, JOHN JAMES MILLAR.—Admitted to practice at the Bahama bar, Easter term, 1862; appointed notary public same year; elected member of the house of assembly, 1866, and on the dissolution of that body in 1869 was re-elected; appointed master in chancery 1869, resigned 1873; admitted to practice at the Turks and Caicos Islands' bar, 1873; commissioned as a notary public; appointed a justice of the peace of the colony, provisional Queen's advocate and escheator general, Oct. 1873; confirmed, 1874; director of public bank, 1874.

THOMPSON, THOMAS AUGUSTUS.—Called to the bar in October, 1872; in Easter term, 1874, received a commission to act as public prosecutor in the criminal court of the Bahamas from the attorney-general of the colony, during his temporary illness acted as the judge of the court of common pleas from June to December, 1875; was appointed police magistrate, Bahamas (being still allowed private practice) in January, 1876; is chairman of managing committee of prisons, and presides over investigations into cases of wreck and other marine casualty.

THOMSON, EDWARD DEAS, K.C.M.G. (1874), C.B. (1856).—Emigrated to New South Wales in 1828, having received from Mr. Huskisson the appointment of clerk of the executive and legislative councils, which he continued to hold until Dec. 31, 1836; on Jan. 1, 1837, he was promoted by Lord Glenelg to the office of colonial secretary and registrar of records of New South Wales, and remained in the exercise of that appointment for nearly twenty years, having resigned it on June 5, 1856, on political grounds, when responsible government was first introduced into the colony. He held a seat in the executive council from Jan. 1, 1837 to Oct. 31, 1859, and in the late legislative council from Jan. 1, 1837 till its dissolution in 1856; since that period he has been a member of the present legislative council or upper house of the legislature; he held a seat in the colonial cabinet, but without office, from Oct. 3, 1856, to Sept. 6, 1857; during which time he was vice-president of the executive council, and in that capacity represented the government in the legislative council; he has been a member of the senate of the Sydney University since its establishment in 1850; and was appointed vice-chancellor of that institution in 1862; he has been a member of the board of trustees of the Australian Museum since its incorporation in 1853; and crown trustee since 1856; appointed chancellor of the Sydney University, 1865.

THURSTON, JOHN BATES.—Entered the British consulate of Fiji and Tonga, January, 1866; was appointed acting consul, July, 1867, and remained in office until December, 1869; upon the resignation of the first finance ministry, was called to office by the united voices of settlers and natives; became chief secretary and minister for foreign relations, May, 1872; March, 1874, was appointed "chosen and special adviser" of the native king and chiefs, to confer with Her Majesty's commissioners as to the annexation of the islands to Great Britain; upon the offer of cession became chief secretary under the " ad interim government, " composed of King Thakombau's nominees and the resident foreign consuls; was again nominated as special adviser to the king and chiefs in September, 1874, for the purpose of conferring with His Excellency Sir Hercules Robinson as to the act of cession; was appointed colonial secretary and auditor-general of the colony, 10th October, 1874.

raw_text: THWAITES, G. H. K., F.L.S., &c.—Was for some time lecturer on botany and vegetable physiology at the school of medicine of Bristol; appointed director of the royal botanic gardens, Paradenia, Ceylon, in June, 1849.
